I am all for the replacement of lead service lines but I believe the EPA needs to mandate if the Utility or customer needs to pay for the replacement. 
Reading the rule revisions it seems like this will become a political hot topic for local governments and put the local governments and their citizens 
against one another.

I agree on the Proposed Rule of the National Primary Drinking Water Regulations: Lead and Copper Rule Revision. I agree because water is vital for human consumptions. By strengthening procedures and requirements related to health protection and implementation of the existing Lead and Copper Rule, the EPA ensures humans are consuming safer water. Growing up we were told not to drink water out of the tap. My mother was getting paid $2 an hour and my dad was making most of the money for the household but was never home. This was the 1990s, and we could not afford a water filtration system nor bottled water on our own. Our life depended on drinking all the water we could at school, or public places. We were afraid that the tap water could potentially kill us. Sometimes, we would wait to get clean water from the food drive right next to our apartments. I think that having harsher regulations in regard to childrens public health is essential. Areas that are at risk for high lead and copper levels should be monitored and reduced.

Water is one of the toughest things to regulate. Currently the 1974 Safe Drinking Water Act mandates standards for the quality of American drinking water. However, with water crises like the ongoing problem in Flint, Michigan, I believe it is imperative that the new proposed lead and copper rule revisions be passed. The water which was originally sourced from Detroit, was switched to the Flint River in an attempt to cut costs due to the vacancy of the car industry from the city. The new water source corroded the pipes and led to an increase in led concentrations as high as 6% above the average in children.
	In Lindsey J. Butlers The Flint, Michigan, Water Crisis: A Case Study in Regulatory Failure and Environmental Injustice she explains how the crisis came to be, but more importantly the social implications of such a disaster. Butler mentions that, the blatant disregard for the public health were, in part, the result of the racial and economic characteristics of Flint. This most definitely played a role in the crisis because a statement released by the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People on January 17th, 2016 read, We must hold accountable Michigan's public officials who chose to balance the city's budget at the expense of the health of the citizens they serve. Even as children were showing up sick in doctor's offices with rashes and cases of hair loss, state environmental officials and elected leaders refused to see the warning signs. Would more have been done, and at a much faster pace, if nearly 40% of Flint residents were not living below the poverty line? The answer is unequivocally yes.
	Butler concludes by stating how in the U.S. there are 6.1 million lead service lines which supply water to 15-22 million people. Some of these lines have been buried over a century ago. Water is being carried, sometimes for miles, through these corroding pipes and being used for drinking, cooking, and bathing. Butler calls for legislation to be improved, strengthened, and actively enforced.
	Stefan Hajkowicz and Kerry Collins describe the process used for making and scoring the overall performance of decision options against objectives in their review: A Review of Multiple Criteria Analysis for Water Resource Planning and Management. Multiple Criteria Analysis (MCA) use a range of equations to take different measurements in different units and convert them into a weight and create a ranking of options. Because water management and planning is an extremely convoluted process, MCA is a great tool to use as a decision support tool in due to the fact that outcomes of decisions are often intangible. The authors write how MCA, has also been shown to improve the auditability, transparency and analytic rigour of water management decisions.
	It is critical that changes be made to the National Primary Drinking Water Regulation for lead and copper. The current regulations are not nearly rigorous enough as crises like Flint are not a one-off occurrence and will only happen more frequently if nothing is done. Furthermore, there needs to be an increase in the use of Multiple Criteria Analysis for water management systems, to ensure the supply of clean, usable water to systems to citizens, regardless of their socioeconomic standings. To not employ these revisions would be an unnecessary tragedy signifying a true disconnect between the American government and the people it is supposed to serve.






Works Cited
Butler, L. J., Scammell, M. K., & Benson, E. B. (2016). The Flint, Michigan, water crisis: a case study in regulatory failure and environmental injustice.Environmental Justice,9(4), 93-97.

Hajkowicz, S., & Collins, K. (2007). A review of multiple criteria analysis for water resource planning and management.Water resources management,21(9), 1553-1566.

Laidlaw, M., Filippelli, G., Sadler, R., Gonzales, C., Ball, A., & Mielke, H. (2016). Childrens blood lead seasonality in flint, Michigan (USA), and soil-sourced lead hazard risks.International journal of environmental research and public health,13(4), 358.

We support the new rule on Lead and Copper, especially for the municipalities that have lead supply and distribution lines. However, the rules also impact many rural schools that have well water and our other schools that have municipal supplied water. 

Thus plans must be made to replace all school buildings built before 1992 and supply federal government funds to the schools.  This is going to drive up local taxes for every resident in the US, especially in the cities where old buildings are still utilized to school our elementary students. Every school building built before 1992 and some up to 1995 construction has lead solder, which was utilized by every plumbing contractor during that time period to install the domestic water systems. The requirement of schools to do testing is going to reveal high lead levels, especially in stagnant distal sites or taps is also going to raise school budgets and thus further increase taxes for the residents of those communities. This is going to be an economic burden to local communities in both large cities and rural communities that many are fighting to provide quality education to our students and also which many are fighting current fiscal disaster. Thus federal funds will have to be supplied to every school district to pay for the testing, which ultimately will require severe tax increases across the board for every US citizen.  I offer the following discussion items that must be thought about with this rule:  

1) Since water is the universal solvent it will leach lead solder and copper into the water during the stagnation time waiting for sampling. This will drive lead levels above the 10 ppb proposed standard for action because the older schools have older  distribution lines with lead solder. 
2) Many schools have older fixtures because the schools have not been remodeled recently. These fixtures most do not have significant levels of lead but most are not lead free. Thus the waiting times are going to cause the schools to replace more taps. 
3) The longer hold times before testing the tap will also increase the lead leaching. The previous 12 hour prior flush of the tap before testing was a good compromise and more realistic hold time before testing and was a better representation of the water quality. Extending the hold times to unlimited length is going to make the taps fail in these older buildings.
4) Rural school buildings who have well water, most being non-transient non-community systems, will also fall under these guidelines and thus also be further impacted because many are doing their own lead and copper testing now and they will fail more often, thus exposing them to further violations from the State EPA's which are responsible for enforcing the laws.       
5) The test protocols to achieve lower levels are not presently available. Lower detection limits are at 2 ppb by most analyses with a significant potential error. The lowering to the 10 ppb limit really does not allow for any error in the testing and for taps that are on the border of passing. What data does the EPA have to say at what level lead is a issue with health. Has medical testing been done on drinking water at 10 ppb to see if it impacts health? 

The consequences of the new law needs to be provided to the american people so they can understand how the new rule is going to impact the local economics for their communities.  I ask that the EPA review the above items and think about the mechanical issues involved with replacing almost every school building in america. 

John J. Farmerie
Cyrus Rice Water Consultant, Inc
Executive Consultant
1200 4th Ave 
Coraopolis, PA 15108
412-269-2468

I strongly support the National Primary Drinking Water Regulations: Proposed Lead and Copper Rule Revisions, which was formulated by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency. I truly believe that this policy can enhance every individuals standard of living. Lead and copper are detrimental chemical elements. Young children can be particularly susceptible to the tragic effects of these toxic elements. Consuming a high level of lead and copper can seriously harm their brain development. As a consequence, reducing the concentrations of these metals in water is of utmost importance. This policy establishes strict guidelines pertaining to water testing. This is exceedingly crucial. By implementing this regulation, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency can help the public gain more access to clean and safe water. This regulation will further improve public health and lead society to become more sustainable.

To Whom It May Concern,

     I am very concerned about the proposed rule revision to the Lead and Copper Rule. The proposed rule revision would decrease this lead service line replacement rate to just 3% annually, which is insufficient. This drop in the pipe replacement rate would extend the life of lead pipes by approximately 20 years.

     Given the many public health crises in recent history resulting from issues around lead in drinking water due in part to existing lead pipe infrastructure, this is not a good idea. The events in Washington, DC in the early 2000s and most notably in Flint, Michigan beginning in 2014 have showed us that in addition to having proper oversight regarding water disinfection and water source changes, lead service line replacement is of the utmost importance.

     In order to ensure superior health conditions for ALL U.S. citizens, we must prioritize lead service line replacement nationwide. The replacement rate should remain at 7%. Peoples lives are at stake.

Thank You,

Danielle Watkins

I believe that this proposed rule by the EPA to revise the NPDWR for stronger protection from lead and copper exposure in drinking water is very important and crucial for public health and well-being. There is still a lot of data out there that shows the dangers of low-level lead exposure and it's adverse health effects on humans, and while some of us have the luxury of getting safe drinking water those in rural areas or poorer cities still, to this day, get lead and copper exposed drinking water. This is unacceptable, as clean water is a basic human right and is totally attainable for the masses, yet the government slacks on this issue. This rule will strengthen existing regulations in the LCR, and with new requirements to test water for lead in communities as well as educating the public, I believe that clean water is only a few steps away. I urge the government to make this proposed rule a reality in order to avoid more unfortunate situations caused by unsafe and undrinkable water in the future.

The dangers of lead and copper are very real to me as a resident of Chicago, Illinois. The recent crisis with Flint's lead poisoning stretched to my high school which caused many of my high school's water fountains to be shut off. I'm not saying that this effect is why I think that regulation of lead and copper is so important, but more so because if it this crisis was able to stretch all the way to Chicago from Flint, Michigan, then the parties that lived within miles of the crisis probably had an extremely rough time. I think that it's really important not to just implement this rule, but also raise consumer awareness and public education of the issue because regulation is always just regulation. It can promote positive behaviors and demote negative ones, but they may still very well occur. People need to be able to distinguish and notice issues with their water from the get-go in order to keep their communities safe.

I'm a strong advocate for this rule and hope that everything goes well!

EPA should require all potable water plumbing appurtenances including backflow prevention assemblies and devices to be properly labeled (Lead Free) on the product, not the container or box that it was packaged. This change will help reduce non lead free plumbing appurtenances from being installed and purchased by the general public and water related industry professionals. There are still big box retailers and plumbing supply houses all across the United States that offer for sale non lead free plumbing appurtenances. The general public often does not know the difference and will usually select the less expensive plumbing appurtenances that do not meet the lead free requirement. 

I would also encourage EPA to take this opportunity to require all backflow prevention related assemblies and devices to meet and be marked with the lead free designation. The backflow cross-connection control industry continues to see backflow prevention assemblies being installed in potable water applications using lead containing assemblies and devices. Examples of this include HBVB's, wet based fire line suppression system and landscape irrigation assemblies. It should not matter if the down stream utilization is intended for non-potable consumption. These backflow prevention assemblies and devices are connected to the potable water supply.

Thank You

I think all lead service lines should be removed. The problem is that the homeowner may not have the funds to do so.  Is there any chance of grant money for these people.

I completely agree with this proposal. Decreasing the amount of lead in our water and making sure that the water we drink is safer and healthier is a goal that we always must strive towards. The quality of water in America is of the utmost importance and it has definitely been questioned after incidents like Flint, Michigan. Taking this step to ensure there is lead content in our waters will go a long way in ensuring clean drinking water for everyone.

I agree with the proposed rule in the sense that it identifies the areas most impacted by lead and water contamination in drinking water. The lead service line inventory maintained by all water systems will work to reduce elevated levels of lead in certain locations, along with identifying the causes of elevated levels. This aspect of the proposal is relevant to the Flint water crisis in which the drinking water for the residents of Flint, Michigan became contaminated with lead after the water source was changed to a less costly part of the Flint River. By utilizing water systems that "find-and-fix" the causes of elevated lead levels in drinking water, we can avoid future crises as seen in Flint. The residents of cities deserve to have access to drinkable water, as it is a right. I also agree with the EPA's proposal to require systems to notify customers within 24 hours of an action level exceedance. This prevents customers from further consuming contaminated water and builds on transparency with the issue. Communicating the issue between the service line and the customers is crucial in preventing residents from becoming sick from elevated lead levels. One of the most important measures of the proposed rule that I strongly encourage is the sampling of drinking water outlets at each school and child care facility by community water systems. I believe the results from these tests can provide schools with a plan of action if their water is found to have high levels of lead. Without these tests, the children at these schools and facilities will unknowingly be consuming contaminated water. I think by actively identifying the sources of lead contamination and not waiting for people to get sick, the EPA will be able to better prevent future crises.

I believe it is very important to implement replacing lead pipe plumbing that have already been in use to eliminate lead from being in drinking water. I understand that this is a very difficult thing to do and am at least relieved at this revision to not continue to install lead pipes in plumbing. Something that stood out to me in the description of this rule is the three Ts which are training, testing, and taking action to continue to eliminate lead and copper from drinking water. This way everyone will be educated and know what to do if lead is detected in water. Strengthening the rules and regulations to keep lead and copper out of drinking water will bring us to more success and continue to lessen the amount of lead detected in blood tests.

Access to clean water is a fundamental right. It is extremely important for people to be able to trust that the water that comes out of the tap is safe to drink. I am encouraged that this rule proposes ways to lower lead levels, especially in the places that are most in need. As there is data showing that even low levels of lead have health impacts, it is crucial to make sure we are doing everything we can to remove lead from our drinking sources and keep it out. Let's even the playing field and set more children up to succeed by removing lead from our drinking water altogether, and not wait for another Flint to make national headlines.

Looking at the notice proposals suite of actions, I would like to comment on the 6th key area that addresses protecting children in schools from lead exposure in their water outlets. The proposal discusses how community water systems (CWS) is going to conduct lead in drinking water testing for 20% of elementary schools that are located in a service area every year. However, I believe that the frequency of this testing should be increased. Instead of testing annually, I firmly believe that it should be tested monthly to accurately ensure that there is no lead in these outlets as it could be very detrimental to a young childs life if found too late.

I believe that this rule is certainly a step in the right direction for the drinking water of this country. We have seen issues with old piping and outdates systems a number of times in this country recently, most memorably in Flint, Michigan. I saw in the summary of this rule that there are a number of revisions being made to the LCR regarding the lead content of water. However, the summary also says that there are no changes being made to the copper content. Why is that? I am probably not as familiar with the issue as many of the people involved in this rule-making process, but I am curios as to why the rule says that it is making drinking water safer by reducing lead and copper and then does not actually reduce the copper content of water.

I am strongly in favor of this proposed rule. With recent tragic events in areas like Flint, Michigan, the issue of water quality is apparent and now public knowledge. I think this is especially important in children. As mentioned in the proposed rule document under 1. General Information, "data evaluated by the National Toxicology Program (NTP), 2012 demonstrates that there is sufficient evidence to conclude that there are adverse health effects associated with low-level lead exposure." Additionally, the World Health Organization claims that any even minuscule lead exposure levels can lead to increased blood pressure and kidney damage in adults. This information, as well as additional information, can be found here: https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/lead-poisoning-and-health. All in all, this is a wonderful proposed rule with clear evidence of a need for a rule like this. By focusing on children, that is especially critical because many children are ignorant of the differences in water quality, especially if there is no visible difference, and because lead exposure can have long-term negative effects that they might not realize until years later.

I think that these regulations on lead and copper are strong, which is more than needed to best protect citizens and ensure our drinking water is actually safe. First touching on lead, according to the Utah Department of Environmental Quality lead is especially a health concern for children since the growing bodies are more susceptible to the harmful effects - which include brain and kidney damage, learning disabilities, behavioral problems, hyperactivity, and impaired growth. This is why I particularly like the fact that this proposal includes stronger testing requirements in schools and child care facilities, since obviously plenty of children are present in these locations. 

While copper is essential for humans, only 2mg per day is recommended and higher doses can cause serious health problems - which can be caused by tainted water supplies that hopefully these regulations will help curtail. Long term overexposure to copper can cause anemia and harm the liver and kidneys. 

Because of both of these clear potential health defects, this proposal should be passed to help ensure the safety of the public. I believe it is a right to have access to clean water, and I would not want to have a government that would not do its best to serve the greater good in such a fundamental thing to life.

Not only is it a good idea to increase quality specifications of water to be considered drinkable, but it is more important the the agency be able to delegate to the state level that it should be their responsibility to conduct ample water testings. Making sure that drinking water is safe in the United States is a main way that we set ourselves apart from 3rd world countries. If we cannot implement tighter reigns on our one necessary fuel, then that is problematic.

As a past student of Chicago Public High schools, I am in heavy support of this ruling. Specifically the part that emphasizes the importance of having filtered water available at all water fountains in public schools. I believe that when it comes to our educational systems, they ought to be one of the top priorities for having the most advanced and effective technologies in terms of air quality, technological capabilities, as well as water purification standards. As a society, we should strive to make all educational zones as safe and state-of-the-art as possible. We appear to be lacking in taking care of our planet in the long run, we might as well try to enhance our children's futures as much as we can. I can still remember the strong metallic taste in the water fountains for much of my childhood, and given the research surrounding the effect of metallic substances in the human body, we ought to strive for the cleanest drinking water possible.

I completely support the proposed revisions to the EPA's current National Primary Drinking Water Regulations. I believe it is imperative to ensure that the drinking water is safe for our communities. The idea of limiting lead in new and existing infrastructure is a great way of ensuring this solution. I always hear in the news about how bad the water quality is in locations such as Flint Michigan. So, I am happy to see the EPA taking actions to guarantee the improvement of this water in the present and the future. This is definitely a win for the Safe Drinking Water Act!

Something that should be taken into account though is going to be the cost of these infrastructure improvements. When identifying the most problem areas across this country, we also need to examine the state of their financials and if they can afford to maintain the quality of drinking water.

I completely agree with the proposed rule. It is very important for government to transparent with the public and I believe the expansion of this rule will effectively accomplish this. The fact communication is one of the key factors the EPA wants to build on emphasizes this. Communicating the issue between the service line and the customers is crucial in preventing residents from becoming sick from elevated lead levels. The piece about the EPA requiring systems to notify consumers is also important to improve communication as well as decrease the chance of lead-related illnesses or deficiencies. 
The fact this rule focuses on protecting school children is also a very important aspect of the rule as lead-related illnesses can cause developmental delays in children. If testing was required in schools, then the risks associated with lead being in drinking water systems would cease entirely and protect the children of our future. Children are one of the few demographics that are especially susceptible to the effects of the slightest amounts of lead as their bodies are still developing and their brains and nervous systems are highly sensitive. Pregnant women are also impacted greatly by lead exposure as lead can be stored in the bones and can be released later, and can therefore expose the child to lead and cause prenatal brain development. 
The area of finding the areas that have the highest levels of lead is a productive process to effectively eliminate the problem. If we identify the areas, we can work to fix the systems that have proven to be problematic. The identification stage also has the potential to become a preventative measure; if there are areas that have not quite reached the level of lead presence that spike concern, but has shown to be increasing slowly over time, then the governments may be inclined to take action before the situation gets out of hand. The pipes would be replaced in a timely manner and could prevent populations from becoming ill. This proposed rule covers both the proactive and reactive aspects of rulemaking, and therefore has the potential to be really beneficial after revisions and reimplementation.

AGENCY: 	Environmental Protection Agency (EPA).
Health Issue: 	Lead in San Francisco's Public School Drinking Water

	The San Francisco Unified School district is endangering the health of school children because the schools' drinking water has unsafe lead levels that are inconsistent to the American Academy of Pediatricians safe requirements. For instance, 136 public schools were tested for lead levels and the data showed that 65 schools had less than one part per billion, 66 schools found they had between one and five parts per billion, and 16 schools had lead levels at over five parts per billions. Therefore, this data illustrates that San Francisco's public schools are not safe for the school children and are exposing them to deadly toxins that will permanently damage their developing brains and cause major health issues, such as, slowed growth and development, learning and behavior problems, and hearing and speech problems.
	The American Academy of Pediatricians have strongly advised on the deadly effects of lead poisoning on young children, that is, even the smallest amount of lead is dangerous for children because "exposure to lead can seriously harm a child's health, including damage to the brain and nervous system, slowed growth and development, learning and behavior problems, and hearing and speech problems." Therefore, EPA needs to intervene on this health related issue to eradicate unhealthy level of lead in San Francisco's public schools and to protect the childrens' health. 
	The problem of having unsafe lead levels in the San Francisco's public schools' drinking water was caused by the federal/state lead level standards which are higher than the safe ones from American Academy of Pediatricians, that is, "the federal/state standard of 15 ppb whereas pediatricians' have a much lower safety limit of 1ppb". This illustrates that the federal/state level standards need to be updated to match with the safer ones of the American Academy of Pediatricians. Therefore, EPA needs to needs propose the change at the federal/state level to ensure that all public schools have safer lead level limits by following the American Academy of Pediatricians'.
	EPA's involvement in changing the federal/state level standards on lead levels is important it is responsible to ensure that lead contamination is controlled at the source by requiring the San Francisco School District to follow the safe guidance on lead levels. The school childrens' health needs to be protected especially lead poisoning has deadly effects on their health that will permanently damage their brains and ruin their lives. 
	Therefore, EPA's involvement is important and one that needs to be addressed faster due to the higher health risks associated. The EPA involvement is important to facilitate the policy change at the federal/state level by using the data that was collected from the San Francisco School District on the lead contamination. By using that data the EPA can propose for different policy changes that can be applied to ensure school children are safe. For instance, mandate the schools to take action by officially asking them to shut down which might cause problems but in the long run it will be safe for the childrens' health. EPA involvement in this policy action needs to be addressed faster.  





References
Calpirg. (2019). Lead Levels in San Francisco Schools. Retrieved from 	https://calpirg.org/feature/cap/get-out-lead-map

Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. (2019). Prevent Children's Exposure to 	Lead. Retrieved from https://www.cdc.gov/features/leadpoisoning/index.html

Lead Levels in San Francisco Schools
Lead Levels in San Francisco Schools

As a publicly funded agency, the EPA must fulfill its mission to protect the public from environmental toxins, especially lead. I strongly urge the EPA to require all water systems to fully replace all lead service lines within a specified timeline. I also demand that the EPA orohibit partial lead service line replacements, which put people's health at risk and are inefficient. Finally, the EPA must closely monitor water systems with elevated lead levels and fully replace lead service lines at a rate of at least 7% each year. I implore the EPA to make sure that the Lead and Copper Rule is effective and visionary, and that EPA robustly supports implementation and enforcement.

The major water crisis in Flint, Michigan isnt only in Michigan. There is an all-time high of having lead in the drinking water in public schools in San Francisco, California right now. Older pipes and plumbing fixtures are what causes to have lead in the water system, therefore, contaminating these public schools. According to the World Health Organizations (WHO) Young children are particularly vulnerable to the toxic effects of lead and can suffer profound and permanent adverse health effects, particularly affecting the development of the brain and nervous system. leaving the children in public schools vulnerable due to lead exposure in their drinking water. A high enough concentration was found at about 403 parts per billion (ppb) at West Portal Elementary School in San Francisco and at Downtown Highschool who had the highest lead concentration found in the water was at 660 ppb. According to The Food and Drug Administration (FDA), lead levels should be below the point of EPA regulation of 15 ppb in drinking water. This is a major concern for not only the people of San Francisco but everyone else in the Bay Area, if not the whole state of California as well. I think its really important to manage this crisis especially amongst those that are high at risk like the children in attending these public schools. Spreading awareness of this issue and implementing the importance of lead monitoring can keep all U.S Citizens safe from lead poisoning. This regulation will further improve public health to provide more access to clean and safe water for the public.

In my current neighborhood, I can check online to see that my apartment has lead service lines. However, testing of water costs between $20 and $100. I am concerned for the health of the children in my building, but don't have the money to foot the cost of the testing. Would you consider adding a provision the Lead and Copper Rule revision addressing costs of testing home drinking water? I feel this is especially pertinent if you have known lead pipes.

I think that this proposed rule is a good idea for the healthy and safety of our country. In this day in age, people are attracting health conditions that lead back to lead. With decreasing lead it will lead to safer conditions. I would also like to ask how with this rule affect the country as a whole? Will it cost a huge amount? Furthermore, with all of these health conditions from unsanitary water this will benefit everyone. Although I believe this will benefit everyone, what proof is there to show for the need of this revision?

Environmental injustice has plagued communities for decades and has left long lasting health issues. I agree to this proposal because it will protect communities who are exposed to lead and copper in drinking water. This a positive step towards the protection of the public and its health. By strengthening procedures and actually testing the drinking water, the government is taking action in addressing water contaminations. Water contamination is one of the largest problems in low-income areas and it leads to many health issues like waterborne diseases. Flint, Michigan has suffered and this proposal will help regulate water sources to ensure that situations like that dont happen again. This regulation will improve public health and ensure clean drinking water for everyone.

I agree with this new proposed rule because the safety of our drinking water is a human need. I believe the safety of water is something that community members should be educated about. After what happened in Flint MI it is important for people to know the threats that are possible in their water. I think it is important to educate the public on the effect of consuming copper and lead in their water and why it is important that they do not ingest these harmful things. I think that more testing and the removal of lead pipes are a great start to ensuring clean and safe drinking water.

In the National Primary Drinking Water Regulations: Proposed Lead and Copper Rule Revisions post is a great proposal for more effective efforts to reduce any copper or lead exposure when ingested by the public. There needs and needed to be a proposal like this so instances like Flint Michigan can cease to exist. The U.S needs increased state and federal efforts to keep the public safe from long term and short term affects of lead and copper exposure. Lead, specifically, worries me the most as there are many associated risk factors that many people, especially in cities and older rural towns, are at risk for exposure. People located in cities have had buildings that are older homes and buildings often retain remnants of this paint. People renovating an older home are at even higher risk(Mayo Clinic). I know of and have seen the communities surrounding downtown Chicago, especially in the south side, are homes built before 1970. These buildings were never torn down because of unpopular value in the rel-estate market, so they would be considered a neglected property or owned by a person with low income. A very low sale on a house that may or may not be up to safe living codes is not unfortunate and unacceptable on a morality perspective. Pipes are never taken out, and the house, in terms of air quality due to household dust can contain lead from lead paint chips or from contaminated soil brought in from outside(Mayo Clinic), there is no safe area around the perimeters of the property. There can even be more congregated too. 

I love the fact that the EPAs efforts in the quality of water. I hope the EPA can take my idea in consideration. I hope the government recognizes this as a very extreme case, and bad for the quality of life in the neighborhoods currently affected in Chicago. Other states also deserve better quality of water, and the removal of lead pipes in homes affected. Those affected across The United States of America live in these poisonous living predicaments deserve every right to be recipients of benefit in the solution to ensure a safe right of living without the worry of being poisoned. This includes those who are given false water quality reports, or they are already given compromised water quality reports. 











Work Cited

Lead Poisoning. Mayo Clinic, Mayo Foundation for Medical Education and Research, 6 Dec. 2016, www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/lead-poisoning/symptoms-causes/syc-20354717.

I believe that this is a beneficial and necessary regulation to implement across the nation. Clean water is an inherent human right and people should not have to be concerned that their water is contaminated with dangerous metals. Though there are previous regulations which diminish the probability of lead and copper exposure, such as the Safe Drinking Water Act of 1974 and the regulation that banned lead pipes in 1986, significant changes must be made in order ensure the safety of the country. It was pointed out that there are still numerous areas in the United States which have outdated plumbing and therefore contaminated water. This regulation will help modernize these areas and guarantee the safety of the people who live there. 
	Prolonged exposure to lead deposits in the water can have hazardous repercussions to public health, especially to the health of children. Bodies of young children and infants more readily absorb lead deposits than adults. This can mean serious impacts to brain and nervous system function. Additionally, these lead deposits significantly decrease the ability of their blood (hemoglobin) to absorb oxygen. Copper contamination has a similar negative effect because it can cause liver and neuronal damage in people with Wilson's disease, as well as gastrointestinal problems in people without this disease. Both lead and copper contamination are known carcinogens and effect children and adults. 
        In seeing the severe symptoms lead and copper contamination causes and the prevalence of contamination in the United States, it was made quite clear to me that this regulation is extremely important and necessary for public health. Should it not be passed, there will be many areas of the United States where infants and children will be consuming contaminated water. This contamination will have lasting effects for their health, and accordingly the health of our nation in the future.

I firmly agree with this propose rule, especially the 6th key area on protecting children in schools. As a California Bay Area native, I am concerned about the high amount of lead contained in drinking fountains in the San Francisco school districts. According to Calpirg.org, schools like Downtown High School have 662 parts per billion or ppb of lead in their water. Whereas, Malcolm X elementary school, West Portal elementary school and New School of San Francisco have lead in their drinking water that ranges from 52.6 ppb to 538 ppb. That number is high above EPAs lead regulation of 15 ppb. 

According to pediatricians and scientist at the American Academy of Pediatrics, any amount of lead, even at 1 ppb, is considered toxic and dangerous to anyone. Lead is a neurotoxin that affects brain developments and learning abilities, limits activity and behavior, impairs hearing and formation, and optimal functioning of blood cells, especially for young children attending these schools. 

Having access to clean and safe drinking water is an ongoing process and therefore EPAs proposed policy has to be stronger and executed more stringently. EPAs lead regulation of 15 ppb at the federal and state level standards by far is not safe enough and needs to be immediately updated and aligned with the American Academy of Pediatrics. 

As EPAs proposed policy moves forward, I would also like to request that the community water system (CWS) sample each school and each childcare facilitys drinking water outlets every month to ensure that there isnt any lead or copper in the outlets. I dont think annual checks will suffice as contamination can happen anytime and safety protocols may become more lenient over long periods of time. But, when you do establish a more frequent safety protocol, you have better quality control and easier safeguarding practices for the children and students from lead contamination.

This rule needs to be totally re-focused.

Nearly all Lead and Copper failures are a result of houses with very old service lines, fixtures, solder etc. This is a result of poor maintenance by the HOME OWNER. These pipes and fixtures would be 70 years old or older. Why are we being forced to take ownership of them?

When the paint manufacturers were regulated, they were not expected to re-paint everyone's house and furniture. It is unfair to impose this expense and responsibility on water utilities.

The water utility should only test for the following: 

1. Lead and Copper coming from their own facilities. 
2. The corrosive qualities in their water. (PH, Alkalinity) 

If the utility has non-aggressive water and tests negative for lead and copper, their responsibility should be over. This is the case nearly all the time.

Instead the Lead and Copper Rule should do this:

1. Make a way for people to get a free test for lead and copper.
2. If they are over the action level, they should be given options as to what they choose to do with their own property.
3. Given options as to how to deal with their problem, since it is due to their property. (Point of use devices, Changing Plumbing or Do Nothing and drink water from another source.
4. They should be required to disclose the test results at time of sale of the home like is required of lead paint.

The utility serving them should be listed as a resource for the home owner and could help them with the process, but SHOULD NOT be responsible for any plumbing owned by the home owner.

There should be no lowered action level.

The 24 hour rule is ridiculous given that the shady research as it is, it takes many years of lead consumption to affect health, if it does at all.

Utilities and State Agencies do not have the resources to implement or enforce these rules.

Schools and Daycares should be responsible for their own facilities and testing. PRIVATE PROPERTY should be handled by the OWNERS. Do you remember what country you are in?

As a publicly funded agency, the EPA must fulfill its obligation to Protect Our Public Health! While the EPA's proposed Lead and Copper Rule is a step in the right direction, it does not go far enough. Specifically, I cite the following requests from Clean Water Action, which include:
Require all water systems to fully replace all lead service lines within a specified timeline.
Prohibit partial lead service line replacements, which put people's health at risk and are inefficient.
Water systems with elevated lead levels should fully replace lead service lines at a rate of at least 7% each year.

I request the agency reexamine their decision-making process regarding the proposed amendments to the National Primary Drinking Water Regulations: Lead and Copper Rule Revisions, Docket ID: EPA-HQ-OW-2017-0300, more specifically their decision to lower the rate of replacement of lead service lines from 7% to 3%. Even if this amendment successfully closes loopholes in the current version, as Administrator Wheeler contends, it seems that keeping the rates at 7% would be that much more beneficial. Maintaining a 7% rate of replacement prevents closing loopholes then merely returning to status quo by lowering the rate.
Studies have shown the admittedly immense costs tend to have even greater benefits when looking at the costs of health care intervention and lost productivity. Elise Gould, although concentrating more specifically on lead paint in the cited research, found the societal costs of lead poisoning on children to be huge, and the investment in reduction worthwhile. She looks at a wide array of effects of childhood exposure to lead. Looking at merely one small facet of the problem, Gould discusses the costs of lead linked ADHD, finding that it costs roughly $267 million annually to treat. 
The paper goes on to discuss the loss of IQ points due to lead poisoning, guessing that the loss of each point costs an average of $17,815. She goes on to discuss the increased crime rates, lowered earnings, cost to society by parents missing work to care for sick children - the list goes on. Her estimates of costs run well into the billions. The potential to speed up remediation of the problem should be embraced, and the proposed brake-pumping would by default at best maintain status quo, and at worst further slow replacement of the pipes. 
Sincerely, 
Alexander Dreussi
Current Law Student, Former Midwesterner, and Concerned Citizen

1.	The EPA will for the first time require a public lead service line Inventory.  The City of St. Petersburg Water Resource's Department (City) has removed all known lead pipes in our system.  It will be extensive and time consuming to try to determine where or if there are any left.  
2.	A galvanized pipe preceded by a lead fitting at any time must be included in the Lead Service Line Inventory requirements.  This change now makes it so that any lead fitting on a service line classifies the entire service line as a "lead line", even though only a small portion contains lead.  If this is the case the city would be responsible to remove and replace at the utilities expense without any confirmation of lead contamination. 
3.	When a customer replaces their owned portion of a line, the City will be required to replace the City owned portion within 45 days. While 45 days may be reasonable in most cases, there should be some flexibility due scheduling and site considerations. 
4.	The EPA is proposing to improve tap sampling procedures such as wide-mouth bottles for collection, prohibiting flushing, cleaning or removing faucet aerators before sampling.   Considerations for false positives need to be made before corrosion control studies are required. 
5.	Currently there has been limited discussion on the true cost impact to the increased sampling.

Outdated and faulty water delivery systems continue to expose millions of U.S. residents to lead in their drinking water. There is no safe level of lead exposure, and the effects of lead exposure can last a lifetime. EPA has stated the need to modernize and improve the existing Lead and Copper Rule to increase public protections against the dangers posed by lead in drinking water in communities throughout the U.S. (1,2). 

The proposed rule, however, would actually slow the replacement of lead-contaminated (lead service lines) LSLs and thus increase the time over which residents could be exposed to lead. The existing rule calls for 7% of LSLs to be replaced annually in community water systems if lead measurements reach EPA's Action Level of >15 ppb. The new rule actually lowers that standard to only 3% of LSLs. This change would effectively more than double the time for replacing offending LSLs for a given water system. A separate assessment by the Natural Resources Defense Council estimated that replacement of all LSLs could take 13 years under the existing rule, but up to 33 years under the revision (3). This assessment called for setting a maximum of 10 years to replace all LSLs nationwide. The American water industry believes that locating and removing all LSLs is urgently necessary (2,3). 

The EPA has also not chosen to decrease its Action Level of 15 ppb lead in drinking water. Although the EPA has proposed decreasing the Trigger Level to 10 ppb, the World Health Organization has stated that 10 ppb is not safe for the most vulnerable, notably children and pregnant mothers and their fetuses (2,3). Moreover, Canada has recently reduced its allowable lead level at the tap, its maximum contaminant level (MCL), to 5 ppb, and Europe has recommended reducing its MCL from 10 to 5 ppb. The NRDC has urged the EPA to lower its lead Action Level to 5 ppm (3). 

Over the years 2015-2018, an estimated 5.5 million U.S. residents were served by water systems exceeding the 15 ppm Action Level, and an estimated 30 million residents received water from systems that were in violation of the LCR in a variety of ways. It is time to prevent another generation of children from growing up drinking lead contaminated water in their homes and schools (2,3). I'm opposed to the new rule and urge the EPA to reconsider and reduce the timeline for replacing lead-contaminated pipes.

(3)   www.nrdc.org/media/2019/191010; www.nrdc.org/experts/erik-d-olson/heres-whats-needed-fix-outdated-epa-lead-tap-water-rule

We appreciate the extension of the comments time frame, otherwise we would not have had the opportunity to voice our opinion.

We are a water only utility with 3,400 connections serving rural customers in Southern Indiana. In the interest of my time I will bullet point some of the problematic areas of the proposed rule, however this is not all encompassing: 

If the customer takes the sample and we are not there, we cannot certify the sample was taken correctly and should not be required to do so.

If there are daycare facilities, the business should be required to take and pay for their results.  As well as address any issues with their water lines that would be causing any issues.

If Lead and Copper were to be sampled separately it would be confusing to track the results and keep them strait as when to sample.  Also it would add to the cost and the time.  They should be sampled together as they are now.

We cannot require our customers to replace their service line if it is causing problems with copper or lead.  Our bylaws state the customer is responsible for their own service line, we can only fix what is on our side. 

Many of the places that would have galvanized lines are lower income areas and do not have the funds, or the desire, to replace their lines.

Customers that do replace their service line do not tell us.  Therefore, we may assume they still have lead lines on their side, when they have already been replaced.

Buying pitchers for each customer for 3 months after replacing a service line is unreasonable.  The customer always has the option to buy a pitcher or filter of any kind, we should not be responsible to do so.

It is not reasonable to require us to educate the customer within 24 hours of a sample if it is high 

There are many more questionable and unreasonable facets in this proposal, but I am limited by time. 

I have only had 2 customers on our system have any issue with lead since I've been here, and after pinpointing the root of the cause, both were paint related-as in most cases.

This proposal is painted with a broad brush and would require many unnecessary requirements for systems that do not have any issues with lead or copper. Just a few of the new proposals would greatly increase the amount of time devoted to lead and copper, which would interfere in many other of the essential operations of water quality.

Dear EPA Administrator, 
As a way to protect public health, the Lead and Copper Rule was created in 1991 by the Environmental Protection Agency. The Lead and Copper Rule regulates the way water systems are monitored. This rule attempts to provide tests and treatments to minimize the likelihood of lead and/or copper in our drinking water. This rule is also part of the Safe Drinking Water Act. The Lead and Copper Rule sets maximum contaminant levels on drinking water.
I understand that the Environmental Protection Agency is proposing to make a few changes to the Lead and Copper Rule. The overall goal is to provide more reliable information on elevated lead levels. There will be more tests being conducted in schools and child care facilities. There will also be more testing in what is considered high risk areas. Homeowners will be notified about any elevated lead levels within 24 hours of the findings. The overall lead monitoring routine will be improved. These efforts are a way to regain the public's trust and fulfill its duties to protect public health and safety. 
I believe that the proposed changes to the Lead and Copper Rule could have its benefits. These changes could prevent future crisis. The more these rules and guidelines are enforced, the more we can avoid tragedies like the one that happened in Flint, Michigan. I am not opposed to the new changes that are being proposed. I like the efforts that the Environmental Protection Agency is making in order to ensure the public's safety. This is a step forward in acknowledging the mistakes that have been made in the past and taking accountability. I do think that there should be more things added to this rule. I would like to see rules that will 100% guarantee that no more residents will be receiving toxic water. The Flint crisis is something that definitely could have been avoided. 
Sincerely,
Danielle Bowden

Considering all the statistics and the effects lead has on adults and infants adverse health, I am in agreement with the proposed regulation on improving lead exposure. There is a lack of testing infants for lead which can lead to catastrophic health failures. According to many different tests, even the slightest exposure to lead, at 1 ppb, you can see symptoms of lead exposure. There is always room for growth, especially with the tools and technology that is constantly evolving. The proposed regulation is indicating extra measures taken in action level/trigger level, tap sampling, corrosion control treatment, notification and public education, lead service line replacement, small system flexibility, school and childcare facility testing and education, and alternative LCR revisions considered. Each different system ensures a more efficient way to detect lead and to mitigate them. This regulation doesn't have any cons and I am in full agreement with the improvising of the existing regulation.

The lead and copper rule is of course designed for community systems.  There are real problems with the way the rule (and presumably the proposed rule) is implemented on small facilities (NNs and small OTMs). 

Inconsistency in PbCu results at individual sampling sites are compounded by the low number of samples collected (5 samples at most NNs OTMs with 90th percentile calculated to include high outliers instead ofeliminating those rare outliers as would be the case with sample sets > 10). This results in ALE exceedences occurring at higher rates than at larger systems.

It is very troubling to see these results are not consistent/reproduceable
high results seem to come out of nowhere and are followed by low results.

It is understood that variables such as how long water has sat in lines, temperature and perhaps how often valves are turned all factor into the results in difficult to measure ways.  These factors add an erratic unscientific tinge to the results which is troubling in the regulatory framework.

The material inventory requirements may resolve some of these problems.  Particularlyat many OTM mobile home park facilties where pvc piping is usedinsteadof metalic piping.

At least half of the ALEs I seeon small systems are for copper.  It is good to seesome effort is being made to separate lead and copper remedies/responses

Similarly to what is occurring in Flint, Michigan pertaining to the ongoing lead issue, there is also a growing lead concern in the public schools of San Francisco. Although most San Francisco schools are in compliance with federal and state standards, it is still a danger to have lead exposure anywhere near children and schools. California Public Interest Research Group (CALPIRG) conducted a lead content test for all 136 San Francisco public schools in 2018 and the results were alarming. CALPIRG then put together an interactive map that displayed the lead testing results. Results showed that 65 schools had less than 1 ppb, 66 schools had between 1 to 5 ppb, and 16 schools had lead content over 5 ppb. Also, pediatricians firmly believe and stand behind the fact that any lead content amount over 1 ppb is deemed to be hazardous. While on the other hand, the government does not agree and deems that anything over 15 ppb is the fatal level. This contrast in beliefs and standards is a huge issue because the government is not openly listening to what medical professionals have been telling them about lead when all the medical evidence is shown and recorded.

Under the Key Area # 6 you, the EPA, are trying to protect our nations schoolchildren from being exposed to lead and other harmful toxins that can be found in drinking water. I do not agree with your proposal to have water system revisions being done progressively. I believe you should revise this key area to ensure immediate actions are being taken to reduce, or even eliminate any traces of toxins like lead and other heavy metals from our drinking water supply. 
	Your current regulation is for 15ppb lead in drinking water, but this comes from extremely outdated information. The Academy of Pediatrics have been urging you to change this antiquated regulation down to 1ppb of lead, because the majority of evidence has lead to the fact that, there is no safe level of blood lead concentration for children. As the EPA you have long been aware of the dangers of lead in the body, and have linked lead exposure in children to reductions in IQ, learning disabilities and even stunted physical growth. 
	With 15ppb lead in drinking water being the regulation, this means that school districts may be under the impression that the drinking water that their students are drinking is safe when in actuality that is far from the truth. Massive proportions of schools across the US are above what is considered safe by prevailing scientific knowledge, however with the current regulations, they are unable to receive funding to make meaningful changes.
	Modern science has evolved, and we need to make sure that our rules and regulations are evolving with them to ensure the safety of the children of our nation.

Eliminating lead from water should be a priority to ensure safe drinking water for children. There is a huge discrepancy in what is considered safe in the federal governments standard versus the American Academy of Pediatrics recommendation. The federal standard of lead in water is 16 parts per billion, while the American Academy of Pediatrics recommends no more than 1 part per billion. Recent studies have proven that there is no safe amount of lead in drinking water (AAP, 2016). The San Francisco unified school district has recently tested their schools drinking water for lead, and the results were shocking. Most of the schools did not meet the recommendations of the American Academy of Pediatrics. Lead affects children the most since they are still developing and it causes harmful health effects that are not reversible. Some of the health effects of lead include cognitive and behavior problems, irritability, fatigue, and abdominal pain. Since the adverse health effects of lead are irreversible, preventing exposure is critical. The first change that should be implemented is that the federal standard should match that of the American Academy of Pediatrics recommendation of no more than 1 part per billion. Next, every school, preschool, and daycare should have their water tested and any place that does not meet the standard of no more than 1 part per billion of lead level in their water must rectify the problem immediately. What that can entail is closing the harmful fountains and providing another source of water until the problem is corrected.

Protecting Community water systems should be a national issue that needs quick intervention. For instance, the San Francisco school district has been testing its drinking water for lead, and a shocking result shows that over 50% of schools in the San Francisco Unified School District (SFUD) have lead present in drinking water. I agree with the EPA proposal to have all water outlets in schools and childcare facilities to provide water samples that can be tested, and the system provides the result information. They should also provide information about the actions the schools or the child care facilities can take to lower the lead in drinking water.  Most schools built before the 2010 lead ban have possible lead water pipes and faucets (EPA, 2014).
Therefore, I agree with EPAs proposal that will require all water that has plumping pipes to have proper labeling so that Americans can quickly identify them when purchasing. Millions of Americans usually look more into the price of the pipes, but they do not look at the brand and the health-friendly materials. I also agree with the proposal that the EPA should notify customers when elevated levels of the lead rise in drinking water to avoid a crisis like what happened in Flint (Taylor, Wright, & Housman, 2016). This will prevent customers from getting sick in the future and also by identifying the sources of lead contamination. Instead of waiting for people to get sick, the EPA can prevent lead from being contaminated in drinking water. Also, a representative should go to different school districts and communities to bring awareness and have people join the advocacy for having the state and federal get abased standard for lead in drinking water. Another way we can help the situation is by improving transparency and communication between EPA and the communities.
 Every community faces different issues with water, and the best way to resolve the lead problem is to get everyone involved. The EPA proposed rule is expected to increase community awareness and further reduce sources of lead through enhanced Lead service lines replacements. We also know that the Federal and State governments allow up to 15 parts per billion, a limit that most medical pediatricians have proved to be very high. The transparency and communication by EPA will allow the community to know the number of parts per billion in their water so that they can be able to decide their lead limit. 
Therefore, I support the proposals by EPA, which include but not limited to identifying areas most impacted, strengthening treatment requirements, replacing Lead Service Lines, increasing sampling reliability, Improving risk communication, and also protecting children in schools.

I am in favor of the revised Lead and Copper Rule. Decreasing lead and copper exposure by enacting stricter action level thresholds is paramount for providing communities with safe drinkable water.

As the rules are currently proposed, if the the water system is notified of the customer-initiated replacement more than 3 months after the replacement occured, then the water system is not required to replace its portion of the LSL. Section 141.84 (d)(5) of the revised rule states "when a water system is notified by the customer that he or she has replaced the customer-owned portion and the replacement has occurred more than three months in the past, the water system is NOT required to complete the lead service line replacement of the system-owned portion." 

Given the length of time it took for the US EPA to issue these revisions, it is wholly unfair for customers who replaced their portion while waiting for LCR revisions can now be further delayed or even denied having the water system portion replaced simply because they were proactive about replacement.   

This section needs revision.

North Logan City has concern over the following proposed rule section: All water systems must develop an Lead Service Line (LSL) inventory or demonstrate absence of LSLs within first 3 years of final rule publication. This requires water systems to identify all unknown service line materials on both sides of every meter up to the house.

 
North Logan City understands the rationale for determining the need to validate there are no lead service lines, requiring physical validation of materials of each service line when available records and historical staff knowledge indicate no lead service lines in our community, would result in an undue burden on the water utility and the home owner with limited benefit to the community.
North Logan City has not maintained a data set of service line material. In North Logan City, all connections past the water meter are the homeowners responsibility. We do not replace, fix, or service any part of the Homeowners service line.
Service lines are underground, and therefore not visible without excavation and/or entry into a customer's dwelling. Service lines are typically not visible in our meter barrels without excavating. 
Even if we did verify all 2600 service connections (We are a small city) That would be a huge undertaking and 3 years is a short time frame for a Utility Department that only has 3 Full Time Employees, and a 7 month dig window due to Winter conditions. 21 months to do 2600 connections puts us at 124 verifications per month. That takes a large amount of time. Even if the service is copper or a poly line, we could not know about a pipe type change halfway through the yard, or lead fittings used, without exposing the entire line. We put our meter in the park strip and a minimum of 4 feet deep to keep them from freezing. That is a lot of fill, sod, and irrigation fixes we would have to undertake.  
Creating a data set of all service lines will require a significant undertaking and expense; requiring excavation and entry into private property. Digging  up someones front yard just to see the service lines will be a negative experience for both the City and the Citizens.

We have removed all Lead "goose necks" tapped to the Main lines that run to the meters. But a data set does not exist as we did this over the last 30 years, before Data sets were even a thing. Staff historical knowledge should suffice.

As evidence by the unfortunate situation in Flint, Michican, and other lesser publicized cities throughout America, the need to updating is crucial.  The impact lead and copper can have on an individual's body, particularly those of today's youth, is a significant price to pay for a problem that can be addressed through regulation and enforcement.

CALPIRG collected data from San Franciscos schools and found that there are schools with levels as high as 660 ppb. This is something concerning for the parents who take their kids to such schools, but also for those who live around the area. In the 6th key area, under General Information, the EPA focuses on protecting kids in schools by reducing their exposure to lead in drink water that is found on the premises of school and childcare facilities. Just recently, I wrote a brief Op-ED on the San Francisco Chronicle, which focused on the levels of lead found in the elementary and high schools in San Francisco (SF). Based off my research from CALPIRG, a data base for the results of lead testing in SF schools, most of the schools seem to comply to the <15ppb, however; there are a few schools within the district that have tested for 400-600ppb. That is up 40 times the federal recommendation. 
According to an article on the American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) website, Lead and Drinking Water: Information for Health Professionals Across the United States, published by the Pediatric Environmental Health Specialty Units (PEHSU), The National Toxicology Program and U.S. Environmental Protection Agencys (US EPA) Lead Integrated Science Assessment both concluded that adverse neurodevelopmental cognitive impacts occur at blood lead levels less than 5 g/dL (https://www.pehsu.net/_Library/facts/LeadandDrinkingWater_62116_final.pdf). By this, we can see that there is a recommended level of ZERO amount of lead that our kids should be getting from drinking water, which contributes to 20% of their blood lead levels.  
So, what do we do about it? The first thing is we need to educate ourselves. The FDA is a great resource and a great place to start. Also, websites such as the ones listed above, CALPIRG and the AAP, have great information on levels of lead the kids in the community are exposed to and the harmful effects that such exposure can cause. Next, implementing and advocating for an uncompromising higher standard of policy when it comes to lead in water. In 2012, the AAP strongly advised the FDA to change its ppb standard from 15ppb to 1ppb, because they recommend ZERO lead in water for children. Lastly, we need to start testing schools and our homes for lead in drinking water, becoming the advocates for the growing minds of our future generations. CALPRIG is only just starting out in San Francisco, so just imagine if ALL schools, ALL over the world had information and access to such indispensable data.

The proposed health policy aims to reduce drinking water's exposure to lead and copper. This initiative also strengthens procedures to measure and protect against lead in community water systems. Although the measures are commendable, some modifications can be done. The policy has focused on six key areas, the most important one being the protection of children in schools. The authors recommend community water systems (CWS) sampling of the drinking water of each childcare facility and school.  Furthermore, this policy area aims to introduce corrective measures to reduce unwanted lead based on the results of the sampling. The major problem of this recommendation is that it is very time consuming, expensive, inefficient, and dangerous. It would take a lot of time and money to make all schools and childcare facilities served by the system. Also, it would require even more time to analyze the samples. If the water is indeed exposed to unwanted lead, this could well have serious implications for the health of the children and the staff consuming the drinking water. An effective suggestion to alleviate this problem would be to focus the time and money on supplying alternative sources of drinking water to these institutions till the samples have been analyzed and corrective actions are taken. The proposed health policy is a very positive step forward by the government to safeguard public and community health by regulating the amount of lead and copper in the drinking water. It will greatly take forward the issue of protection of drinking water. Public health should be the topmost priority for the society.

Lead contamination in water has always been a dangerous problem especially if children are the ones being exposed to it. Being exposed to lead is extremely dangerous to our bodies. Lead is highly toxic to our immune system it can damage our brain, kidneys, liver, blood and reproductive system. It can also cause lower IQ, anxiety, depression, and ADHD, which can have a bigger impact on a child because it'll hurt their learning process in school. 
According to CALPIRG, in the San Francisco school district, 50% of its schools have tested positive for lead contamination in their water. This is a big concern for those who have kids because they are exposed to toxic chemicals during their education hours. According to the American Academy of Pediatrics, any amount of lead is dangerous for people especially for children because they are still developing.
This proposed rule would help to keep children safe from drinking water fountains in their schools. Although I agree that schools should be required to provide results about lead contamination in their facility, EPA should do more than just provide information to schools on what steps to take to reduce lead in the water. Schools should also be required to fix the problem or else they would be fined. EPA should provide incentives that would actually make the school take action in a certain amount of time from when they tested their facilities water. Whether it be a fine the facility would get for not taking action or a kind of reward they get if they do fix the problem. Anything that helps schools to take action and reduce lead contamination in the drinking water and helps children to be safer in school should be added to the EPAs proposed rule.

San Francisco California Water Crisis, Lead Crisis in Drinking Water Affecting Thousands of Children in California, and Children in California Consuming Contaminated Water will be the next trending headline in 2020 if circumstances are left idle. We need to protect our children and to do so, more needs to be done than simply testing outlets at each school and educating them on how to reduce levels of lead. By lowering the safe level of lead in drinking water to 1 part per billion or lower, this would significantly affect the outcome for the better. Modern science shows that even low levels of lead can impair the brain development of fetuses, infants, and young children (Denchak, 2019). Though many schools in San Francisco are well below the safe federal level of lead, 15 parts per billion, there are many schools well over the safe level in the hundred zone. All schools should be tested for lead levels of contamination and addressed accordingly.

Incorporating Schools and Day Care facilities should not be included in the revised Lead and Copper Rule.  Schools and Day Care facilities need to be regulated by their own regulating body.  School Districts have their own environmental sections that already have the capabilities of testing their water system.  By adding this to the Lead and Copper Rule, it puts undue stress and costs on a workforce that already is finding difficulty in meeting current regulation requirements, especially for smaller utilities.  Schools and Day Care facilities can start their day at 4:00 to 5:00 am which makes it difficult to sample according to the required sampling procedures.  

Another item that the new regulation needs to take into account is that residential faucets with a single water handle is becoming more common which has a potential to contaminate a cold water sample with hot water since there is only rubber O-rings that separate the hot and cold water discharges into a single flow.  Along with the new residential faucets are longer necks after the operating level. If the customer uses hot water prior to the sampling event, then water that has been heated can stay in the neck of the faucet causing sample contamination from water that was previously heated.

Water suppliers should have the capability of follow-up sampling with a customer that detects lead higher than the "Action Level".  We have had experience with previous customer requested sampling that shows one faucet in the household having lead levels higher than the "Action Level" but other faucets in the household having lower levels of lead than the "Action Level".  

Water supplies should have the capability of rotating their identified Lead and Copper sampling sites.  Our customer sampling database volunteer to be on the sampling list.  Without the capability of rotating, we have customer's asking why their residence does not get sampled.

When it comes to the cleanliness of our water supply, there are various factors that we have to take into consideration. A heavy metal that has severe adverse effects is lead, and we must regulate it within our water supply. The population who is more vulnerable to lead exposure are children; they can develop an acute illness that will affect them not just physically, yet mentally. The American Academy of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry stated that Exposure to lead can have a wide range of effects on a child's development and behavior. Even when exposed to small amounts of lead levels, children may appear inattentive, hyperactive, and irritable. Children with greater lead levels may also have problems with learning and reading, delayed growth, and hearing loss. At high levels, lead can cause permanent brain damage and even death. (American Academy of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry, 2019) With this said, it is essential to ensure that areas such as schools and childcare facilities are adequately maintained.

             A pragmatic first step would be to inspect the plumbing of the buildings; the most common pipes used in plumbing are copper and galvanized pipe. They are considered heavy metals, which could potentially be released into our drinking water. There needs to be transparency with the facilities and the public with what is occurring. In the article, Lead found in more than half of San Franciscos public schools, states that, "The American Academy of Pediatrics has recommended that we really limit lead levels to no more than one part per billion" (Melendez, 2018) This statistic is ideal because they are opposing the statement of federal and state-level standards. Pediatricians have a more cautious perspective on what could be better or improve the health of children. We need to take their medical statements significantly into consideration due to their license and expertise. Another proposal could be by implementing new technology such as installing lead filtration systems. Adding these to facilities such as schools and childcare facilities it would deplete the amount of lead that would generally be present in their water. By taking these proposals into account of improving the ways, the EPA could potentially reduce the amount of lead in our water supply by adamantly advising others of these solutions.
