The proportion of people in developing countries with access to safe water has improved, yet in our own developed, rich country, theres thousands of innocent children exposed to danger.  According to the California Public Interest Research Group (CALPIRG) over 50% of schools in San Francisco Unified School District have water sources with unsafe levels of lead.  Lead is a highly potent neurotoxin even at low levels which causes IQ loss, anxiety, depression and ADHD. Even low levels of lead have significant effects on childrens brain development, on their attention levels, on behavior and on their ability to learn. 
The Federal standard for safe levels of lead in water is 15 parts per billion, but according to the American Academy of Pediatrics that should be 1 part per billion.  However, the Center for Disease Control says there is no blood lead level for children that can be considered safe. 
San Francisco Unified School District is not doing enough to protect children against lead contaminated drinking water.  Its appalling that 136 schools were found to have more than 1 part per billion, and they continue to expose the children to the lead. As a school employee, I know how innocent these children are, they trust the adults to have their best interest at heart. We are failing our children by not taking action to correct this problem.
The EPA needs to require the community water systems (CWS) sample drinking water outlets at each school and each childcare facility served by the system. The results should be given to the schools and facilities along with information about the actions the school or childcare facility can take to reduce lead in drinking water. If any high levels of lead are found, immediate action should be taken.  Which should include, requiring schools to inform parents of lead levels found over 1ppb, turning off the unsafe water fountains and advising students not to drink the water, Encourage students to bring their own water bottles from home and having clean water bottles available for students in need.  If the EPA creates these new mandates, requiring all schools to have their water tested, it will help save many innocent children from lead exposure. 
The Federal Government needs to pass a bill that would provide the necessary funding to California schools to replace the old lead contaminated pipes and bring them up to par with stricter water regulations. 
We are in in charge of looking after our childrens health and safety, if we dont, they will continue to be unknowingly exposed to poison. And we knowingly allow it. What a shame on us!

From our background on this issue, we have identified three key flaws with EPA's proposal. Specifically, it:
1.Ignores lead service lines,
2.Relies on inadequate sampling, and
3.Does not provide sufficient support for remediation.
We also are concerned that the result of this proposed rule may sound like "one hand clapping." If state licensing agencies and local health departments are not requiring or promoting testing, child care facilities are unlikely to cooperate, making it more difficult for CWSs to comply with the requirement. For this requirement to have greatest effect, CWSs need the support and participation of all parties involved.
Why not recommend that LSLs be identified and removed first, followed by sampling at each drinking water tap to identify lead sources internal to the building. Without first replacing the LSL, the first draw testing results will not provide useful information to pinpoint whether the lead is coming from the LSL or a fixture. This calls into question EPA's proposed sampling approach which ignores the presence of an LSL.
EPA could address these issues by requiring CWSs to cross reference the LSL inventory and schools/child care list to both prioritize LSL replacements in such locations and inform the testing schedule to ensure testing occurs after LSL replacement where possible. Ideally the appropriate state agency would make public a list of all schools and licensed child care facilities with LSLs; however, EPA does not have the authority under the SDWA to require this.
EPA's proposal runs contrary to its own voluntary guidance, 3Ts for Reducing Lead in Drinking Water Toolkit, that recommends that all outlets possibly used for water consumption be tested, including any "sink known to be or visibly used for consumption (e.g., coffeemaker or cups are nearby)," as well as a follow-up 30 second flush sample if the first draw sample is elevated.[4] It is also inconsistent with many state testing requirements, which commonly mandate sampling at every drinking water outlet.
EPA's Economic Analysis provides no rationale for the agency's decision to use such a limited sampling scheme. Presumably the agency's decision was made to limit cost. However, much of the effort and cost associated with sampling for the CWSs revolves around getting into the facility in the first place. EPA estimates CWSs will spend 144.8 minutes (2.4 hours) per facility - identifying the facilities, coordinating sampling schedule and logistics, traveling to the facility, conducting facility walk-throughs, etc. - before sampling begins. After such an effort, it makes little sense to conduct such limited, arbitrary sampling. In contrast, EPA estimates 10 minutes to collect a sample and $21.58 for analysis by a commercial lab. 
EPA's proposal would place the burden of fixing high levels of lead on the schools and child care facilities. CWSs would only be required to provide facilities with the testing results along with EPA's 3Ts toolkit.

While the 3Ts toolkit is a helpful resource - with instructions for more detailed sampling, remediation options, and templates for communication with parents - ultimately facility staff will be fully responsible for determining when there is a problem and then identifying and financing appropriate solutions. From our experience, child care facilities need significant support to understand testing results and remediation options. CWSs have critical contextual information (such as local water quality or cost of remediation options like flushing or LSL replacement), that could help facilities respond to testing results in a thoughtful manner and avoid wasteful efforts. EPA failed to carefully consider what types of technical assistance the CWS may be able to offer. Further, schools and child care facilities would not be required to remediate based on sampling, as EPA does not have the authority under SDWA to regulate these entities.
In an effort to fill a regulatory gap, EPA proposed requiring CWSs to ensure water is tested for lead at all schools and licensed child care facilities through its LCR revisions. While an important objective, water testing at these facilities will be a challenge without the support and participation of a wide variety of actors - including the state and local education and licensing authorities, health departments, and the schools and child cares themselves.

Where these critical partners are absent, the result may be like the sound of one hand clapping. This may well happen in many states because, under SDWA, EPA only has the authority to regulate water utilities - not schools, child care facilities, or any of the other essential players. We encourage other federal agencies and national organizations to consider how to support EPA's objective, such as through updating national child care recommended guidelines (e.g., Caring for Our Children Basics, NRC Caring for Our Children national standards).

Dear EPA Administrator Andrew Wheeler:

As a concerned American citizen, I strongly urge you to address the deficiencies in the Proposed Lead and Copper Rule Revisions.  Lead represents a serious public health threat.  We know that exposure to lead is particularly toxic to children and developing fetuses, causing serious, irreversible damage to their developing brains and nervous systems.  Lead is also linked to fertility issues, cardiovascular disease, and kidney problems in otherwise healthy adults.   

We also know how to reduce lead at the tap, and now is the time to do everything we can to get lead out of contact with our drinking water.  Rather than weakening its Lead and Copper Rule, the EPA should require all water systems to fully replace all lead service lines within a specified timeline.  Water systems with elevated lead levels should fully replace lead service lines at a rate of at least 7% each year and partial lead service line replacements, which put people's health at risk and are inefficient, should be prohibited

I ask that you please take this opportunity to make sure that the Lead and Copper Rule is effective and visionary, and that EPA robustly supports its implementation and enforcement.  The people of this country can afford no less.

Thank you for your consideration of this important matter.

Dear Administrator Wheeler,
My group appreciates the opportunity to provide input to the Environmental Protection Agency's November 13, 2019, Federal Register notice, proposing revisions to the Lead and Copper Rule.  
Our group is called Common Sense in Federal government.  The lead and copper rule is important to us because lead at ppb levels in drinking water has no known health effect and yet there is much burdensome regulation around it and you are making it more so with these changes]
Lead is a very stable element and lead in drinking water has not been shown by the EPA to be harmful at levels of 0.1 ppm over a span as little as 10 years.
In fact empirical evidence from children who grew up in suburbs all across the USA in the 50's and 60's shows no liver or brain effects from lead in drinking water.
Is Lead  in drinking water is a well-recognized public health concern or has this been blown out of proportion and slurred with the effects of lead on other sources.    Success to-date to control environmental lead exposures are a tremendous success story, including the reduction of lead in drinking water accomplished under the Safe Drinking Water Act.  As a nation we think enough progress has been made in eliminating lead exposure.
We think that population centers that work towards identifying lead piping and then apply CCT to their water with a pH adjustment will be able to get the 90th percentile below 50 ppb which should be more than sufficient based on costs to keep the public from any adverse health effects of lead from drinking water.  Lead from other sources is rare these days in America so why work so hard to remove all lead from water when overall lead exposure is deminimus. 
Water systems are balancing multiple planning objectives at present part of which is  working to integrate lead risk reduction measures without impairing achieving other priorities.  Raising the lead action level to a number such as 100 ppb would help this effort.
In finalizing the rule, it is important that the Agency:
1.	Clearly state in the rule that lead service line inventories are intended to guide proactive actions based on available information like that described in the proposed rule and additional data collected in the course of routine water system activities. Moreover, water systems, or portions therein, constructed following the Safe Drinking Water Act Lead Ban or other state or local policies with like effect, should be able to certify that lead service lines are not present rather than complete the inventory-related activities in the rule.
2.	Water systems should be prepared to assist schools and childcare facilities in the community(ies) they serve upon request. Lead exposure in K - 12 schools and childcare facilities is important and should be addressed by those facilities.  My friends and I  support action based on EPA's Memorandum of Understanding on Reducing Lead Levels in Drinking Water in Schools and Child Care Facilities with sector associations and its federal agency partners.  The best strategy to address lead in schools and childcare facilities is through a holistic risk reduction strategy as described in the Federal Action Plan to Reduce Childhood Lead Exposures and Associated Health Impacts which should be implemented through the Department of Education, Health and Human Services, and EPA in collaboration with relevant state agencies.  
3.	Corrosion control is a critical tool in managing lead in drinking water that has been implemented.  EPA should drop all changes in this rule to corrosion control techniques.  Corrosion control optimization should not be forced on the states and water purveyors from the DEP.  The only requirement should be to meet a raised 90th percentile level and an attempt made to sample at locations where lead may be at higher levels in the piping if any such buildings exist in any given water system.
4.	The public notification for an ALE should be raised to a 90th percentile lead level above 95 g/L since we know that even at this level there is no serious adverse health effect of lead in drinking water and there are no other major sources or places where any member of the population can be exposed to high levels of lead.
5.	If EPA is not going to identify a 90th percentile lead level above 15 g/L that poses a serious adverse heath effect due to short-term exposure, then the associated Tier 1 public notice and associated risk communication must be very carefully gauged as to not cause undue public panic.  

Best regards, 


Paul McKeon 
Retired Water Utility Manager
Common Sense in Federal government

Comments for Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2017-0300, Long-Term Revisions to Lead and Copper Rule

Thank you for the opportunity to submit comments for this important revision. Although I appreciate the efforts at protecting public health, there are aspects of this rule and revision that are confusing, extraneous, and unnecessary, especially in light of potentially more pressing health-related water quality issues. While I agree that lead lines are an issue in some areas, it is a regionally-specific issue that not all municipalities are concerned with. Many municipalities are more concerned with bacterial contamination, nitrate, PFAS, toxic algae, aging infrastructure, work-force issues, water shortages or lack of storage, or many other numbers of problems that the water industry is facing today. To require all utilities across America to sink innumerable dollars and time into this one issue, that they may not even have a problem with, is foolish and shortsighted. Further, blood lead levels in children have decreased dramatically over the last 30 years, thanks to the combined effort among industries to reduce lead exposure. Water is only one, typically small, avenue of exposure to lead. Putting this unfair burden on all drinking water utilities across America to chase down a tiny remaining percentage of lead reduction is wasteful. According to CDC, in Colorado in 2016, less than 1% of children tested had up to 10 ug/dL BLL, and less than 0.2% had between 10- 15 ug/dL BLL, the remaining percentage were non-detect. Other avenues of lead exposure include paint still found in and around homes, on dishes, toys, iron fences, etc. 

Instead of expending copious resources on this single issue, water utilities must be planning and saving for treatment improvements and upgrades to deal with emerging contaminants and other locally-pressing issues. The new lead rules should apply only to those municipalities with known lead water mains and should contain a means to certify out of many of the requirements if none exist. Many communities in the western US did not even exist prior to the 1960s (or even 5 years ago in some areas!). To have to annually certify that they do not have lead water mains and continue to have hundreds of residents sample annually or semi-annually is unnecessary and burdensome.
The Lead (and Copper?) Rule is also an unfair burden on water utilities to assume the financial responsibility of homeowners who need to update their own internal plumbing or fixtures. This is not the responsibility or jurisdiction of the majority of utilities. While the utility may be able to provide assistance in determining plumbing materials for unknowledgeable homeowners (if they even care), that should be the extent of liability for the utilities. Plumbing materials should be discovered and disclosed through real estate processes, just as the homeowner learns the build year, exterior and roofing materials, paint history, radon exposure, and structural issues of a home during an inspection process. 

Just as homeowners internal plumbing and fixture materials are not the responsibility of the water utility, nor should schools and daycare businesses be. Again, utilities can be available to offer assistance with discovery and sampling, but it is not within the utilities jurisdiction or budget to replace internal plumbing and fixtures at schools and businesses. 

If the EPA truly wants to rid America of lead, then they need to stop allowing leaded products to enter this country, in any amount or form. They need to fund awareness campaigns particularly to at-risk regions and provide financial assistance for homeowners and utilities to replace KNOWN leaded lines. Do not force utilities across America to spend millions of dollars annually attempting to chase down a problem that may not exist for some, when many utilities can better utilize their resources for other issues. When the final rule is promulgated, please ensure that it is unambiguous, comprehensible, financially feasible, with achievable positive results. Thank you.

The proposed revisions to the existing Lead and Copper Rule to reduce exposure by regulating lead tap sampling, consumer awareness, and public education is absolutely necessary. It has been proven that lead can cause damaging health risks, especially in children. After reading that the EPA suggests that 20% of lead exposure is due to drinking water, I believe it is extremely important to focus on sampling drinking water outlets at children's schools to reduce lead in the water. However, I also believe it is important to note that these revisions do not dive deeper into further regulating copper exposure. While the effects do not specifically target adolescents, I think it is important to further regulate both for public health and the safety of all people. Additionally, I think that the Lead Service Line Replacement proposal is a great idea, but I am unsure how it can be fully implemented by the water systems. There are lots of logistics with the labor for that process and financial aspects, but steps do need to be taken eventually lead up to LSLR.

I support more and better regulation of lead levels, especially in schools, but PRIVATE schools should be included in this as well.  There should be support for public schools that need funds to repair systems with too much lead.

The extra steps the EPA is proposing are essential to the health and welfare of those affected. Some of the key aspects that this proposal seeks to implement are identifying those that are affected and getting them involved to educate them as to how they may be effected and how to help fix the problem. It is one thing to outlaw the use of lead and copper plumbing of this nature, but that law does not assist those that already have the corrosive plumbing. Getting the state and local authorities and the property owners involved may cause more work and cost more money, but it is an extra step needed to prevent the harmful effects of consuming water contaminated by copper or lead.

- For any pH adjustment alternative, the Rule should specifically state that removal of DBP precursors should be considered in conjunction with increased pH in the distribution system so that utilities are less likely to disregard increased pH as a realistic alternative for corrosion control. For systems that may have tetravalent lead scales, maintaining those highly insoluble scales with a higher pH may be preferred over converting to a phosphate-based inhibitor. 

- POU filters should not be limited to pitcher filters. In fact, faucet filters have been known to perform better in NSF testing and in the testing that was performed in Newark as faucet filters are designed to assume a greater allowable headloss over pitcher filters since pitcher filters have very little head to operate with.

- The initial LSL inventory number determines the number of LSLs to be replaced per year when a utility has an exceedance (i.e. 3% of the initial LSL inventory number - including all unknowns as proposed by the revised Rule). When unknown services are verified to be non-lead, will the initial LSL number be updated on a rolling basis and, therefore, the number of replacements will be constantly updated? If a utility assumes all of their unknown customer-side service lines to be lead, the initial inventory count can be drastically greater than the actual number of LSLs which could inflate the 3% replacement number. If a utility gets a violation for not replacing 3% annually and later on determines their initial LSL number was significantly less, will the violation be rescinded? Recommend increasing the percentage and counting verifications (both sides) to a certain extend (limited to a percentage of the total). The initial number of LSLs + unknowns would not change significantly in this case throughout the life of the program.

- Recommend evaluating WQPs and lead tap samples by pressure zone and water quality zones. An optimal WQP range may vary throughout a system.

- Recommend including a 5th liter in tap sampling to capture the lead contribution from the LSL. However, an action level should not be applied to the 5th liter without first obtaining data from many systems to determine what a reasonable expectation of lead levels in the service line with optimized CCT should be.

- Copper monitoring should be separate from lead monitoring and target newer construction with new copper piping. 

- A 24 hour requirement to notify customers is very aggressive and challenging. Recommend a more reasonable, but still very timely, 72 hour window to notify customers.

- With the variability inherent in customer-led sampling and the unpredictability of particulate lead, I strongly agree that individual tap samples with high lead values in a system where the 90th percentile is below the trigger level of 10 ppb should not warrant a review of CCT. I agree with the other provisions of find-and-fix including the resampling and a survey of the home to identify the cause.

- Consider a separate pool for copper. Worst case for copper is not going to be reflected in the proposed new lead sampling pools.
I strongly believe that decreasing the required percentage of pipe to be replaced annually is a terrible idea. It should be mandatory to test levels in schools and day cares.

The proposed rule will be beneficial to the well being of children in disadvantaged communities, such as Flint, where lead contamination in drinking water continues to cause brain and nervous system damage for them. However, the proposed rule does not address how it will fix the issue at hand in the long term, and I believe it would be more beneficial to remove the lead and copper pipes to prevent further contamination. In addition, the rule only requires local agencies to test 20% of schools in a specified area. This is unacceptable and this should be modified to all schools because the health of children is at stake. It should be an obvious decision to invest in public health and replace the lead and copper pipes and to test every school to ensure that the drinking water is safe for the children. Therefore, I advise that the rule be revised to be more preventative and adjusted to fix the issue in the long term.

1. According to this report Non-Transient, Non-Community Water Systems are being given three alternatives to comply with the proposed lower trigger warning 10 g/L. If smaller non communal water users are given the choice to partially or fully postpone action until the higher level of 15 g/L is reached they will inevitably delay action to save money. These alternative options should be removed and instead grants should be more heavily allocated towards non communal water users. The report states: "...Congress appropriated $50 million in FY2018, to fund grants to small and disadvantaged communities for developing and maintaining infrastructure, for lead reduction projects, and to support the voluntary testing of drinking water in schools and child care centers." Smaller disadvantaged communities should have increased outreach and funding to comply with this new regulation fully instead of using alternatives to allow them to stay stagnant in their environmental efforts. Smaller communities are a vulnerable population and due to low income being linked to less comprehensive healthcare it is of the utmost importance that any regulation enhancing health and safety is enforced to the fullest extent in these areas. Lessening their requirement is counterproductive.

2. As of 1967 the Clean Water act was amended to include an annual water quality report from Community Water Systems, known as the Consumer Confidence Report. This report is being proposed to have new and more comprehensive language to warn people of the negative effects lead can have on children especially. The language reads: "Exposure to lead can cause serious health effects in all age groups. Infants and children who drink water containing lead could have decreases in IQ and attention span and increases in learning and behavior problems. Lead exposure among women who are pregnant increases prenatal risks. Lead exposure among women who later become pregnant has similar risks if lead stored in the mother's bones is released during pregnancy. Recent science suggests that adults who drink water containing lead have increased risks of heart disease, high blood pressure, kidney or nervous system problems." This language should also be mandated to be distributed to primary care physicians of families who have young children or are expecting children. Children and developing fetuses are most prone to harm due to lead and the prevention of these effects should be emphasized just as much as the remediation that this trigger warning offers. Parents will be more prone to beware of lead if they find out from their doctor versus a water quality report that there is 1) no guarantee consumers will read and 2) will not be shown to non-communal water users.

There is no safe level of lead exposure, yet outdated and faulty water delivery systems continue to expose millions of U.S. residents to lead in their drinking water. EPA has stated the need to modernize and improve the existing Lead and Copper Rule to increase public protections against the dangers posed by lead in drinking water in communities throughout the U.S. (1,2). The proposed revisions focus on a variety of factors including identification of lead in drinking water, methods of implementation of protective or corrective actions, replacement of lead service lines (LSLs) bringing water into homes, schools and day care centers, and notification and education of the public on appropriate actions to take.

As stated by the EPA and other organizations, the primary problem with lead contamination in drinking water lies with the estimated 6 million LSLs bringing contaminated water to residents of the U.S. (2,3). The proposed rule, however, would actually slow the replacement of lead-contaminated LSLs and thus increase the time frame over which residents could, and likely would, be exposed to lead (4). The existing rule calls for 7% of LSLs to be replaced annually in community water systems if lead measurements reach EPA's Action Level of >15 ppb. In contrast, the new rule calls for only 3% of LSLs to be replaced when >15 ppb lead is found. This change would effectively more than double the time for replacing offending LSLs for a given water system. A separate assessment by the Natural Resources Defense Council estimated that replacement of all LSLs could take 13 years under the existing rule, but up to 33 years under the revision (5). This assessment called for setting a maximum of 10 years to replace all LSLs nationwide. The American water industry believes that locating and removing all LSLs is urgently necessary (2,5). We agree that achieving this within 10 years is a worthy goal.

It is disconcerting that EPA has chosen not to decrease its Action Level of 15 ppb lead in drinking water. Although EPA has proposed decreasing the Trigger Level to 10 ppb (the level at which water systems must develop plans for the possible reaching of the 15 ppb Action Level in the future), the World Health Organization has stated that 10 ppb is not safe for the most vulnerable, notably children and pregnant mothers and their fetuses (2,5). Moreover, Canada has recently reduced its allowable lead level at the tap, its maximum contaminant level (MCL), to 5 ppb, and Europe has recommended reducing its MCL from 10 to 5 ppb. The NRDC has urged the EPA to lower its lead Action Level to 5 ppm (5). We strongly support this change.

The singular reason for reducing allowable lead concentrations in water now is to prevent exposure to lead, rather than wait for elevated lead to be detected and then for remedial actions to be instituted (5). Remedial actions take time. LSLs are removed only when such remedial actions are ineffective in reducing lead sufficiently. People are exposed in the meantime. The effects of these exposures can last a lifetime (6).

It is widely accepted that there is no safe level of exposure to lead. Yet, over the years 2015-2018, an estimated 5.5 million U.S. residents were served by water systems exceeding the 15 ppm Action Level, and an estimated 30 million residents received water from systems that were in violation of the LCR in a variety of ways. It is time to prevent another generation of children from growing up drinking lead contaminated water in their homes and schools (2,4).

I am writing to opposed the proposed lead and copper rule revisions, which would slow down the replacement of lead-contaminated lead service lines (LSLs) and thus increase lead exposure. The existing rule calls for 7% of LSLs to be replaced annually in community water systems if lead measurements reach EPA's Action Level of >15 ppb. However, the new rule stipulates that only 3% of LSLs be replaced when >15 ppb lead is found. 

Essentially, this change would increase the time for LSL replacement by over 100%. An evaluation by the Natural Resources Defense Council estimated that replacement of all LSLs could take 13 years under the existing rule--but up to 33 years under the revision. That assessment proposed a maximum of 10 years to replace all LSLs nationwide. 

It is also disturbing that the EPA has chosen not to decrease its Action Level of 15 ppb lead in drinking water. Although it has proposed decreasing the Trigger Level to 10 ppb (the level at which water systems must develop plans for the possible reaching of the 15 ppb Action Level in the future), the World Health Organization has stated that 10 ppb is not safe for the most vulnerable-- children and pregnant mothers and their fetuses. Canada recently reduced its allowable lead level of tap water to 5 ppb, and Europe has recommended doing the same. 

Reducing allowable lead concentrations in water now would prevent exposure to lead rather than wait for elevated lead to be detected and then for remedial actions to be taken. 

There is actually no safe level of exposure to lead. Yet, over the years 2015-2018, over five million U.S. residents were served by water systems exceeding the 15 ppm Action Level, and an estimated 30 million residents drank water from systems that were in violation. 

We owe it to our children and their children to take action now to minimize and eliminate lead exposure.  I strongly urge that the proposed rule change be denied.

It is absolutely unconscionable that the EPA  is considering a change in the rule controlling the percentage of lead service lines that must be replaced annually that will reduce the number of dangerous pipes.  No levels of lead are safe.  Safe, clean drinking water is a critical and basic government services that should be available to everyone in this advanced country.  Lowering the percentage to 3% from the current 7% will damage the long-term health of far too many of our citizens.  The National Resources Defense Council estimates it will take 13 years to replace all of the lead service lines under the current rule, but 33 years under the proposed change.  That's nearly 2 generations of children who could be permanently damaged.  I am thoroughly opposed to any change that lengthens the time anyone living in the United States could potentially be exposed to lead.

I am shocked and appalled that the government that is supposed to protect the people of America are even considering decreasing regulations of lead and copper in our water supply. The regulations are there because we learned about the dangers through experience, through children being exposed to lead poisoning. Exposure to lead is particularly toxic to children and developing fetuses, causing serious, irreversible damage to their developing brains and nervous systems. Lead is linked to fertility issues, cardiovascular disease, and kidney problems in otherwise healthy adults.

There are no safe levels of lead and copper in our drinking water. Rather than weakening smart environmental regulations, the EPA should be strengthening them. Let's get and keep the lead and copper out of our water!

Submitted by Indivisible Montgomery, a grassroots organization of nearly 1,700 concerned citizens of Maryland.

The Pacific Water Quality Association (PWQA) supports the option to allow community water systems serving 10,000 people and non-transient non-community water systems (NTNCWS) to elect to maintain point-of-use (POY) devices certified to remove lead in place of corrosion control treatment. 

Numerous studies have been published on the effectiveness of using point-of-use devices to mitigate lead in drinking water, and there is also a place of Point of Entry (POE) devices in quickly and affordably solving corrosion-related water quality issues.

The PWQA further supports ongoing efforts to provide communication and useful information to water system customers. As proposed, providing an annual letter to customers with lead service lines or unknown materials will further promote line replacement. It will also notify customers of their service line and potential options along with replacement, such as certified point-of-use (POU) and point-of-entry (POE) devices to remove lead.

The Pacific Water Quality Association applauds all efforts to reduce lead and other toxic contaminants in public drinking water wherever possible, while standing ready with immediate solutions, such as the use of certified point-of-use and point-of-entry devices, along with certified professionals to ensure products are properly selected, installed, and maintained.


Greg Reyneke, President - Pacific Water Quality Association  www.pwqa.org

Park City Municipal Corporation, Utah, requests that the requirement for water systems to identify all unknown service line materials on both sides of every meter up to the house be reconsidered as proposed. 

	While Park City understands the rationale for determining the need to validate there are no lead service lines, requiring physical validation of materials of each service line when available records and historical staff knowledge indicate no lead service lines in our community, would result in an undue burden on the water utility and the home owner with limited benefit to the community.
	Park City has not maintained a data set of service line material. But rather has used historical knowledge of Building Dept and Water Dept staff to determine there is very little risk of lead service lines.  
	Service lines are underground, and therefore not visible without excavation and/or entry into a customers dwelling. Service lines are typically not visible in our meter pits without excavating fill materials in the pit.  
	Creating a data set of all service lines will require a significant undertaking and expense; requiring excavation and entry into private property.

Opinion/ Recommendations
It has been well known that lead in drinking water is a health risk for more than a century. This issue was also addressed with the Safe Drinking Water Act nearly fifty years ago. The first Lead and Copper Rule came out nearly thirty years ago. The same towns have the same risks from the same water pipes and appurtenances today that were identified in 1991. With these proposed changes, the actual problem is still being deferred to future generations. Within these revisions, water systems are to be required to maintain complex and fragile scales in pipes to protect the public from lead leaching into their drinking water. While water purveyors take pride in providing the safe drinking water that our customers rely on, maintaining scale in pipes is counterintuitive to our objective of keeping water flowing efficiently. We want to minimize scale or growth in pipes. We want efficient and predictable water flow. Also, most water systems do not have the expertise or resources to meet these rule changes effectively. We do not have scientists and chemists on staff to manage portions of the proposed rules and we do not have the cooperation of the public. Even with all of the proposed rules followed, we still have no control over private plumbing systems and what changes are made to the water quality in them. Instead of spending millions of dollars testing and monitoring for lead that we already know is a potential problem, we should remove the lead and not allow it to be a risk to generations in the future. 

In my opinion this is what should be done:

	Require Water Systems to identify water services.

	Require water systems to replace or arrange for the FULL replacement of the service line WITH A DEDICATED DRINKING WATER TAP INSIDE THE HOME JUST AFTER THE MAIN VALVE TO BE PIPED TO A DEDICATED DRINKING WATER FAUCET thereby eliminated the risk of drinking water through the home plumbing system.

	Facilitate/support the authority of water systems to discontinue the supply of water to a residence until the required replacement of the private portion of the water service line including the DEDICATED DRINKING WATER TAP just past the main valve is completed.

	USEPA should make grant money available to finance the private portion of the water service lines INLUDING THE DEDICATED DRINKING WATER TAP if the owner can demonstrate financial hardship to the water system.


	 Eliminate the inefficient lead testing program at homes by water purveyors. It is burdensome, inefficient and unpopular with the public. It is more and more difficult to gain proper sites and cooperation. US or State EPA staff should participate in testing or studies that they feel are worthwhile.

	Devote resources to the many other challenges facing Public Water Systems.

Andreas W. Eddy

Ruiz of CA stated, "The health and safety of our children, not some cost benefit analysis should be the target of our [EPA Lead and Copper Rule.]"

The proposed revisions focus on a variety of factors including identification of lead in drinking water, methods of implementation of protective or corrective actions, replacement of lead service lines (LSLs) bringing water into homes, schools and day care centers, and notification and education of the public on appropriate actions to take.

As stated by the EPA and other organizations, the primary problem with lead contamination in drinking water lies with the estimated 6 million LSLs bringing contaminated water to residents of the U.S. (2,3). The proposed rule, however, would actually slow the replacement of lead-contaminated LSLs and thus increase the time frame over which residents could, and likely would, be exposed to lead (4). The existing rule calls for 7% of LSLs to be replaced annually in community water systems if lead measurements reach EPA's Action Level of >15 ppb. In contrast, the new rule calls for only 3% of LSLs to be replaced when >15 ppb lead is found. This change would effectively more than double the time for replacing offending LSLs for a given water system. A separate assessment by the Natural Resources Defense Council estimated that replacement of all LSLs could take 13 years under the existing rule, but up to 33 years under the revision (5). This assessment called for setting a maximum of 10 years to replace all LSLs nationwide. The American water industry believes that locating and removing all LSLs is urgently necessary (2,5). We agree that achieving this within 10 years is a worthy goal.

It is disconcerting that EPA has chosen not to decrease its Action Level of 15 ppb lead in drinking water. Although EPA has proposed decreasing the Trigger Level to 10 ppb (the level at which water systems must develop plans for the possible reaching of the 15 ppb Action Level in the future), the World Health Organization has stated that 10 ppb is not safe for the most vulnerable, notably children and pregnant mothers and their fetuses (2,5). Moreover, Canada has recently reduced its allowable lead level at the tap, its maximum contaminant level (MCL), to 5 ppb, and Europe has recommended reducing its MCL from 10 to 5 ppb. The NRDC has urged the EPA to lower its lead Action Level to 5 ppm (5). We strongly support this change.

The singular reason for reducing allowable lead concentrations in water now is to prevent exposure to lead, rather than wait for elevated lead to be detected and then for remedial actions to be instituted (5). Remedial actions take time. LSLs are removed only when such remedial actions are ineffective in reducing lead sufficiently. People are exposed in the meantime. The effects of these exposures can last a lifetime (6).

It is widely accepted that there is no safe level of exposure to lead. Yet, over the years 2015-2018, an estimated 5.5 million U.S. residents were served by water systems exceeding the 15 ppm Action Level, and an estimated 30 million residents received water from systems that were in violation of the LCR in a variety of ways. It is time to prevent another generation of children from growing up drinking lead contaminated water in their homes and schools (2,4).

Dear Administrator Wheeler,
The New Jersey State League of Municipalities ("League") appreciates the opportunity to provide input to the Environmental Protection Agency's ("EPA") November 13, 2019 Federal Register notice, proposing revisions to the Lead and Copper Rule.  
The League is a voluntary association created to help communities do a better job of self-government through pooling information resources and brain power.  We have been authorized by state statute, and since 1915, have been serving local officials throughout the State of New Jersey.  All 565 municipalities are members of the League.  Over 560 mayors and 13,000 elected and appointed officials are entitled to all of the benefits and privileges of the League.   
Lead in drinking water is a well-recognized public health concern.  Accordingly, it is the shared responsibility of water systems, customers, consumers, state regulators, federal agencies, local governments and many others to develop policies and plans to address this concern.  It is the duty of each of entities and individuals to take steps within their authority to reduce lead in water. 
To that end, the League has worked with local and state officials to develop a plan to effectively, efficiently, and expeditiously address this issue.  In this endeavor common concerns from the perspective of municipal water systems have cropped up.  The League respectfully asks that, in finalizing the Lead and Copper Rule, the EPA consider these concerns:
1.	Clearly state in the rule that lead service line inventories are intended to guide proactive actions based on available information like that described in the proposed rule and additional date collected in the course of routine water system activities. Moreover, water systems, or portions therein, constructed following the Safe Drinking Water Act Lead Ban or other state or local policies with like effect, should be able to certify that lead service lines are not present rather than complete the inventory-related activities in the rule.  Lead service line inventories are further complicated by the split-nature of the ownership over such lines.  Expeditiously completing and maintaining an accurate inventory of customer owned service lines entails significant resources. 

2.	Corrosion control is a critical tool in managing lead in drinking water that must be implemented taking many more considerations than are reflected in the proposed rule into account.  EPA must allow water systems to identify and manage corrosion control using an array of tools to support timely and cost-effective decision-making rather than the rigid framework proposed.  Evaluation of corrosion control should begin with an assessment of whether the system is currently implementing its corrosion control strategy effectively rather than immediately revisiting what corrosion control strategy is appropriate.

3.	If the EPA is not going to identify a 90th percentile lead level above 15 ppb that poses a serious adverse health effect due to short-term exposure, then the associated Tier 1 public notice and associated risk communication must be carefully gauged as to not cause undue public panic.

4.	Finally, EPA must consider how small and medium communities are going to fund these additional requirements.  While the goal of every municipal water system is to produce the best quality water for their consumers, these systems are also dealing with personnel shortages, limited funding, and aging infrastructure, which all have a direct influence on water quality and should be considered when placing deadlines on some of the proposed requirements. 

Very truly yours,

Frank Marshall
Associate General Counsel
New Jersey State League of Municipalities

The proposed regulation changes to the Lead and Copper Rule continues to place private and public Utility personnel at a 
major disadvantage in terms of monitoring and controlling the Utility's potable water system. Utilities are forced to monitor 
water system quality within private homes, structures and piping which are well outside the control of the Utility. Public Utility 
personnel are faced with legal opinions from State and Parish administrators that prohibit access to the private home and 
business once past the public servitude-EPA/LDH continues to hold the public and private utility responsible for private 
property and structures.  
A more reasonable approach would be to allow private and public utilities to terminate their responsibility for potable 
water quality at the service valve, as allowed by most Public Service organizations.
Also asking homeowners to sample their water and submit their sample to the utility for handling not only provides increased 
responsibility/liability for the utility, but it also decreases accuracy and allows for false data. Utilities should be allowed to 
monitor the quality of their potable water system using sample points identified, approved and utilized by LDH for other EPA 
program sampling requirements. Each utility system is accountable for its potable water quality and is monitored for compliance
by LDH using specific approved sampling sites. The Lead and Copper program relies on untrained and uninformed
homeowners for collection of samples and places the responsibility for a non-compliant property on the Utility.
This program should be operated as all other potable system programs and stay within the legal boundary of the Utility.

III. E.Compliance Alternatives for a Lead Action Level Exceedance for Small Community Water Systems and Non-Transient, Non-Community Water Systems

I agree with the implementation of cost efficient alternatives for small Community Water Systems since this will allow small/low income communities with fewer financial resources to still minimize the impact of lead exposure. However, the recommendation system in which the small CWS's need approval by the state on the implementation of their preferred alternative is flawed. Under the current proposed regulation should the lead action levels exceed in a water system within the six month period after the initial lead trigger level, the small CWS must implement the State-approved compliance option. Having to resort to a State-approved compliance option may not be in the best interest of a small CWS which might not have the resources available to implement a general State-approved compliance option in time to prevent a crisis. One solution might be to empower these small CWS's to  implement their preferred alternatives of CCT, POU, or a LSLR after at most two months of the initial lead trigger level. Giving states a large window of six months to approve a recommendation may result in the needs of a small community being ignored for half a year which is enough to negatively impact a community even with simply exceeding the lead trigger levels.

This proposal does well with the alternates that are being offered for small and water systems. The alternatives will certainly help them despite being financially handicapped. The allowing of lead line replacements would be a very beneficial alternative to communities, especially if it the number being replaced is relatively low. Also, the approved schedule of replacing the lines being a maximum of 15 years also allows for maintained healthy drinking water. The alternative for allowing Point-Of-Use devices would be beneficial as well, especially for much smaller systems. There of course would be extra inconvenience to the homeowners, but clean water surely outweighs it.

Dear Andrew Wheeler,

	The Environmental Protection Agency is proposing six new general requirements for public water systems, provisions to improve water sampling, providing the general public with better information on the risks of lead in their drinking water, and identifying the levels of lead in drinking water in schools and daycare facilities. The six new general requirements include identifying areas most impacted, which require water systems to prepare and update a publicly available inventory of lead service lines, as well as, requiring water systems to fin-and-fix sources of lead when a sample exceeds 15 ppb. Strengthen treatment requirements, which require corrosion control treatment based on tap sampling results and establishing a new trigger level of 10 ppb. Replace lead service lines, which requires water systems to replace the water system-owned portion of a lead service line when a customer decides to replace their portion of the line. Increase sampling reliability, which water systems will follow new sampling procedures and adjust sampling sites to better target locations with higher lead levels. Improve risk communication, which requires water systems to notify customers within 24-hours if a sample collected in their home is above 15 ppb. Finally, better protection for schools and childcare facilities, which will require water systems to take drinking water samples from both schools and childcares facilities served by the system.
	I do not approve of the new proposal because it is not as effective as it seems. "It fails to take the most important step, which is the removal of about 6 million lead service lines that still remain underground throughout the nation," said environmental advocates in a Washington Post article. Unless all the remaining lead service lines are replaced, lead contamination problems will continue to occur. The new proposal is now required to notify homeowners of elevated lead levels by water utility finds, within 24-hours. The Lead Copper Rule, adopted in 1991 by the Environmental Protection Agency, also states that if the level for lead is exceeded, water systems must issue public education materials, at a minimum within 30-days after the system learns of tap monitoring results. The EPA should not lower the lead service line replacement from 7% to 3%, because the new proposal does not pose a reason to lower the annual replacement rate. If the EPA noted that data indicates a possibility of a disproportioned high human health risk from lead in drinking water among minority and low-income populations, then those areas should be treated first, as well as, high risk homes that have lead service lines. The EPA also plans to implement a new trigger level of 10 ppb, when the LCR states that it does not mandate drinking water to stay below the maximum lead or copper levels, due to contaminants entering drinking water after it has left the water treatment plant.

Sincerely,

Sergio Pacheco Cruz

I am submitting comments from  the perspective of a large public water system as defined by the Rule:

Water has become expensive for customers  of many water systems, particularly large systems, where regulatory agencies continue to add regulatory requirements without additional funding.  Every new regulatory requirement is an unfunded mandate.  While water quality is important water supply has become a critical issue as well, increasing the cost of water as wells are drilled deeper and communities litigate over water rights. Water Systems are overburdened and customers are having trouble paying their water bill.  Water professionals and local governing bodies are well acquainted with this serious problem.  If EPA plans to double the cost of the Lead and Copper Program with the proposed Rule there needs to be associated funding from outside of the water system.

The Rule should not require Community Water Systems to collect samples from schools and childcare facilities.  This is an unfunded mandate and creates an unnecessary burden. Schools and Childcare facilities should be required to sample for lead and copper as part of renewing a license or continuing to receive funding or approval  to operate.  They can use an approved lab and the lab can send the results directly to the regulatory agency.  Advice and assistance with corrective action should be provided by the EPA, State, or licensed plumber. Most water systems have exceeded their ability to handle existing mandates and still provide water to customers at affordable rates.  Unless we are taking some requirements away please do not add more .

Promoting the use of phosphate/phosphorous based corrosion control inhibitors can negatively affect wastewater treatment plants that have phosphorous reduction requirements in their permit. Promoting the use of phosphate/phosphorous based corrosion control inhibitors can also impact receiving waters for those plants that have no reduction requirement.  An increase in phosphorous in receiving waters has the potential to create algal blooms and algal toxins. Phosphate/phosphorous also has been shown to promote bacterial growth in water distribution systems.  The proposed rule requires evaluating  1.0 and 3.0 ppm of orthophosphate  for all corrosion control studies.  Will the use of higher levels of phosphorous containing corrosion control inhibitors promote biofilm development and increase the populations of pathogens such as MAC, Pseudomonas, and Legionella that have been shown to be present in premise plumbing? Has EPA considered  "unintended consequences"  to assure that more problems are not being created by this "solution".

The proposed rule requires water systems to replace customer portions of service lines and perform investigation of the source of lead in private residences where individual results exceed 15 ppm for lead.  Replacement  of customer/private plumbing and fixtures should not be required of the water system.   This requirement is costly to other rate payers and carries with it liability for the water system.  Customer claims for damages and repairs not done to the satisfaction of customers can cause undue financial  hardship.  Those having experience with customer complaints can easily forsee issues as small as scratches on countertops or the unintentional killing of a favorite shrub consuming valuable resources. Private plumbing issues are not and should not be the responsibility of the water system.


The proposed rule requires water systems that add a source of water such as a new well to complete additional monitoring.  This is unnecessary if the new well is equivalent to existing system wells - same aquifer, same pumping level, water quality results are similar. The provision to demonstrate equivalence should be included in the proposed rule.

1 - There is no discussion in the preamble to the proposed rule about whether and how the rule would fix the various problems with the current regulations which contributed to the debacle in Flint.  In particular, Michigan state officials misinterpreted the current regulations as not requiring maintaining corrosion control treatment when switching to a new water source, and not prohibiting flushing prior to sampling.  While the state interpretations were wrong, the EPA stated in a Nov. 3, 2015 guidance memo that there were differing possible interpretations of the existing regulations - thus acknowledging that the current regulations needed to be made clearer.  Also, in that guidance memo, the EPA recommended that water systems notify the state agency whenever a switch to a new water source was contemplated - but did not require this, as the current regulations contain a loop-hole. That is, the current regulations do not require such notification when a source currently employing corrosion control switches to a new water source.  It appears that these various problems are being addressed by the proposed regulations.  But the Agency ought to confirm this and explain in the preamble to the final regulations how each and every problem with the current regulations uncovered during the Flint crisis has been fully addressed.   

2 - The provisions in the proposed rule regarding lead service line replacements fall well below what is needed to fully address lead contamination of drinking water.  As has long acknowledged by EPA, corrosion control does not always work.   Corrosion control is a bandaid approach, not a good long term solution. Also, even when corrosion control brings the lead levels in drinking water down to low levels, there is still a health risk from even those low levels of lead. Yet, the proposed rule generally will require lead service line replacements only when the action or trigger levels are exceeded, and even then only at a slow pace (e.g., 3% per year even when the action level has been exceeded).  In contrast, Germany has implemented a program which requires the pro-active replacement of all drinking water lead service lines - See https://www.umweltbundesamt.de/en/press/pressinformation/drinking-water-to-become-lead-free-new-standard-for.  It seems that the EPA remains hesitant to require a program like that in Germany, because in the absence of significant federal funding, lead service line replacement in this country generally is an unfunded mandate for which the costs will be imposed on local ratepayers.  Promptly replacing all lead service lines in poorer communities could impose a significant local burden.  But there is a solution to this problem - the creation of a federal grants program providing real financial support.  While whether to create such a program may be beyond the scope of this rule, it is not beyond what should be advocated for by the EPA Administrator.  Thus this comment should be forwarded for appropriate consideration. There was a time when the federal government strongly supported environmental protection through serious funding support as well as strong mandates.  In particular, the 1972 Clean Water Act authorized federal grant funding for 75% of the capital costs of sewage treatment plants.  In comparison, the current levels of federal support for drinking water systems are grossly insufficient.  The loan funds that are available do little to promote full lead service line replacement, as such loans need to be paid back by the local communities. As a pediatric physician and father of young children, I am deeply concerned with many aspects of this proposal.  I am supportive of lowering the trigger level to 10 ppb.  However, it must be remembered that there is NO safe level of lead exposure.  Therefore, the proposal to continue a 15 ppb trigger for remediation, coupled with extension of the associated timeline to take corrective action, is NOT ACCEPTABLE in terms of the increased incremental exposure that will occur.  Lead is a devastating and well-understood toxin that has devastating effects on both physical and cognitive childhood development, which unfairly and disproportionately impacts communities of lower socioeconomic status.  Quantifiable decreases in IQ; school performance and other cognitive measures; increased attentional dysfunction, aggression, and delinquency; bone loss and weakness; hearing impairment or deafness; kidney impairment; and effects on the developing unborn fetus - these are just some of the many devastating effects that occur with even low-level chronic exposure to lead, far below the parameters set forth in this proposed rule. By extending the timeline allowed for remediation, you are directly increasing lead contamination exposure time, and thereby inherently and unnecessarily increasing the detrimental effects of lead in the population. Knowingly increasing incremental exposure a morally and ethically unacceptable endeavor that directly contradicts EPA's stated mission of "protecting human health and the environment." Furthermore it has broad negative economic consequences, both in terms of increased health system expenditures, as well as decreased productivity resulting from declines in cognitive and school performance and subsequent career potential. While leaving the 10 ppb threshold intact, I call on EPA to remove the unjustifiable extension of remediation timelines in the proposed rule.

Docket No. EPA-HQ-OW-2017-033
I am a physician, board certified in pediatrics with a special certificate of expertise in medical toxicology and almost 50 years of involvement with lead exposed children and adults.  
I am pleased that EPA took the time to study the issue and to promulgate new regulations.  I am concerned however, that these new proposed regulations are not strong enough, they do not reflect the changes in the knowledge we have, or of another agency's approach to childhood lead exposure/poisoning (United States' Centers for Disease Control, aka CDC).
We now know that a chronic exposure to low levels of lead are more damaging to the intellectual development of children than an occasional higher exposure.  That is why the CDC now looks at the statistical 97.5% of children's blood lead levels to set a "reference level" to be targeted by the public health infrastructure.  That level is presently set at 5 mcg/dl and has been proposed to be reset to 3 mcg/dl.  That level is "achievable" and reflects the research into what level of lead can, over time, lead to reduced intellectual function.  Unfortunately, according to both models proposed by EPA for calculating body burdens of lead ( The All Age Model for Lead Exposure (AAML) and the Integrated Exposure Uptake Biokinetic (IEUBK) model), and presented at an EPA sponsored Webinar in the fall of 2018, if a toddler is exposed daily to water with a lead concentration of 15 ppb, the resultant blood lead level would already be near or at the proposed CDC reference level of 3 mcg/dl.  
I have always been concerned about the way the EPA action level is enforced.  Stating that a water system meets the standard when 90% of the specimens tested fall under whatever value is used, results in the possibility that 10% of the population is be "allowed" to be exposed to potentially serious lead concentrations.  In one study done for the state of California, not one of 500 water systems had levels above 5 ppb; thus, a lower standard is possible, both in action level as well as percentage of tested samples needed to meet or exceed that value.
The CDC made the mistake many years ago of dividing their definition of cases by blood lead levels.  That led to great confusion.  I am afraid that the proposed regulation which now includes a separate "trigger level," of 10 ppb requiring further testing will produce a similar confusion.  That trigger level is above the level predicted to result in a toddler's blood lead being at or above the CDC reference level.  Requiring further testing and no remediation except, perhaps, requiring corrosion control, which should probably always be required regardless of lead level, does not truly protect the citizens who continue being exposed to the lead in the water.
I was disappointed by the encouragement of water testing rather than using available funds to remediate areas already known to need such.  Most states have a good idea where the existing dangerous plumbing exists.  States with cooperative CDC agreements already have access to the addresses and zipcodes of children found to have elevated blood lead levels.  Those areas should be targeted for evaluation, including soil and water lead levels and remediated.  The serious problem is to find a way to fund the remediation needed.  EPA regulations are fine up to the plumbing that is privately owned.  A significant proportion of lead contamination of drinking water is contributed within the confines of a private home.  The pipes and the fixtures themselves may need to be replaced.  The rules concerning how fixtures, such as faucets, can be made are relatively recent and older, potentially contaminated ones, may still be available for purchase.  The EPA regulation should be stronger as to the replacement of such.  
The area of public education is mentioned in the regulation.  It is, indeed, necessary to alert the public as to the dangers of lead in water.  Currently, the onus of responsibility is more on the individual citizen him/herself, than on the public health structure.  The first step in alerting the public should be the publication and provision of information about the age and construction of the water mains.  The potential for contamination should be made known to all citizens.  The information provided should stress remediation rather than testing.  
I trust that my remarks can be taken constructively and may help lead to a safer and healthier environment for children, our most precious "possessions," and only hope for the future!

Professor Emeritus
Departments of Emergency Medicine, Pediatrics, Preventive Medicine and Community Health
New Jersey Medical School

Submitted by Indivisible Montgomery, a grassroots organization of nearly 1,700 concerned citizens of Maryland.

There is no safe level of lead exposure, yet outdated and faulty water delivery systems continue to expose millions of U.S. residents to lead in their drinking water. EPA has stated the need to modernize and improve the existing Lead and Copper Rule to increase public protections against the dangers posed by lead in drinking water in communities throughout the U.S. (1,2). The proposed revisions focus on a variety of factors including identification of lead in drinking water, methods of implementation of protective or corrective actions, replacement of lead service lines (LSLs) bringing water into homes, schools and day care centers, and notification and education of the public on appropriate actions to take.

As stated by the EPA and other organizations, the primary problem with lead contamination in drinking water lies with the estimated 6 million LSLs bringing contaminated water to residents of the U.S. (2,3). The proposed rule, however, would actually slow the replacement of lead-contaminated LSLs and thus increase the time frame over which residents could, and likely would, be exposed to lead (4). The existing rule calls for 7% of LSLs to be replaced annually in community water systems if lead measurements reach EPA's Action Level of >15 ppb. In contrast, the new rule calls for only 3% of LSLs to be replaced when >15 ppb lead is found. This change would effectively more than double the time for replacing offending LSLs for a given water system. A separate assessment by the Natural Resources Defense Council estimated that replacement of all LSLs could take 13 years under the existing rule, but up to 33 years under the revision (5). This assessment called for setting a maximum of 10 years to replace all LSLs nationwide. The American water industry believes that locating and removing all LSLs is urgently necessary (2,5). We agree that achieving this within 10 years is a worthy goal.

It is disconcerting that EPA has chosen not to decrease its Action Level of 15 ppb lead in drinking water. Although EPA has proposed decreasing the Trigger Level to 10 ppb (the level at which water systems must develop plans for the possible reaching of the 15 ppb Action Level in the future), the World Health Organization has stated that 10 ppb is not safe for the most vulnerable, notably children and pregnant mothers and their fetuses (2,5). Moreover, Canada has recently reduced its allowable lead level at the tap, its maximum contaminant level (MCL), to 5 ppb, and Europe has recommended reducing its MCL from 10 to 5 ppb. The NRDC has urged the EPA to lower its lead Action Level to 5 ppm (5). We strongly support this change.

The singular reason for reducing allowable lead concentrations in water now is to prevent exposure to lead, rather than wait for elevated lead to be detected and then for remedial actions to be instituted (5). Remedial actions take time. LSLs are removed only when such remedial actions are ineffective in reducing lead sufficiently. People are exposed in the meantime. The effects of these exposures can last a lifetime (6).

It is widely accepted that there is no safe level of exposure to lead. Yet, over the years 2015-2018, an estimated 5.5 million U.S. residents were served by water systems exceeding the 15 ppm Action Level, and an estimated 30 million residents received water from systems that were in violation of the LCR in a variety of ways. It is time to prevent another generation of children from growing up drinking lead contaminated water in their homes and schools (2,4).

(1)    www.epa.gov/newsreleases/epa-proposes-updates-lead-and-copper-rule-better-protect-children-and-risk-0

(2)  www.sciline.org/evidence-blog/lead-drinking-water

(3) www.washingtonpost.com/climate-environment/2019/10/10/first-time-decades-epa-is-overhauling-how-communities-must-test-lead-water/

(4)  www.nytimes.com/2019/10/10/climate/epa-lead-water-pipes.html

(5)   www.nrdc.org/media/2019/191010; www.nrdc.org/experts/erik-d-olson/heres-whats-needed-fix-outdated-epa-lead-tap-water-rule

(6)    CDC's Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report August 10, 2012, 61(04): 1-9.

(7)   Lead Poisoning, Annual Review of Medicine, 55: 209-222, 2004

I think this was a good revision. Copper and lead both have some pretty severe health impacts. Any revision to improve the health standards of drinking water is welcomed by all.

The use of orthophosphates as a form of corrosion control treatment poses an imminent risk to ecosystems subject to waste water disposal by WWTPs. As stated in Section VI.C.9, WWTPs are effective in reducing phosphorus load by 59% before reaching water bodies. The argument being made that only localized impacts may occur establishes a dangerous precedent that this percentage is acceptable to contribute to these localized water bodies. Efforts should be taken by WWTPs to further mitigate the amount of phosphorus reaching these water bodies, however will likely present an increased cost in phosphorus removal in addition to removal costs already incurred. As such, using orthophosphates as a form of CCT should be reconsidered to be a final option for drinking water systems that consistently report above the action level. Alternative CCT measures, including silicate-based corrosion inhibitors, ought to be prioritized as to reduce the impacts on ecosystems exposed to effluent of WWTPs. Furthermore, the use of calcium carbonate as a CCT measure requires further investigation before being entirely removed as an option for CCT. Though some studies indicate calcium carbonate's inability to efficiently reduce leaching of heavy metals, further investigation should be conducted before negating its use as a CCT.

Requiring schools and licensed childcare services yearly would be an improvement over the current policy of test, however, it will be ineffective to place a required yearly test if only 20% of these facilities are tested. It should also be added that the required test must be done within 24 hours following any replacement of additions to the main water line. Any change in tap monitoring should both require Primary Agency approval and publicly announce changes before any changes are made. Under the proposed "Find and Fix" changes a timeline should be put in place to ensure any test results exceeding the maximum amount of lead in the water are confirmed and an action plan on what and when the fix will be made.

Dear Administrator Wheeler, 

I support Clean Water Action's push for the EPA to address deficiencies in the Proposed Lead and Copper Rule Revisions. I have personally tested my own water sources for its total dissolved solids and I was floored by how close my water system is to being outside of the EPA's regulations. We do not want to have Flint Water Crises across the U.S. due to negligent policies.

I especially urge you to consider the Clean Water Action's goals to:

 Require all water systems to fully replace all lead service lines within a specified timeline

 Prohibit partial lead service line replacements, which put people's health at risk and are inefficient

 Water systems with elevated lead levels should fully replace lead service lines at a rate of at least 7% each year.

Please make sure that the Lead and Copper Rule is effective and visionary, and that EPA robustly supports implementation and enforcement.

There is an obvious draw back on things such as cost and dependability, but nobody has put into concern the "push' for the new knowledge. The government is pushing for this new prototype even though the technology needed to make it happen has not been created yet. When new technology is rushed while being built it tends to fail. There is already a need/want for more efficient automobiles so therefor companies already have an incentive to create these futuristic designs. I could see the government forcing businesses to throw out designs that are not ready and this will only negatively affect all parties involved. The environment, people, and market will get where it needs to be as soon as it's is possible and the government forcing for quicker results will not progress anything. I don't expect the government to let off and allow free market work to its full potential. Yet, I ask that when putting these new laws into place they include a rel deadline that will benefit everyone.

I think the overall goals and highlights of this proposal are excellent steps to take for the protection of human health.  Firstly, I think it is wonderful that this proposal includes broader use of corrosion control because corrosion control is important for reducing lead contamination from pipes and can even strengthen pipes over time.  I also like that this proposal mentions the importance of educating people about lead and copper contamination because it can help the general public to understand the risks of exposure and also actions that can be taken to reduce exposure.  Finally, I also think it is wonderful that requirements will be stricter in schools; while it is important to protect children from metal contamination in all drinking water, it is especially necessary to monitor lead and metal levels in areas where children are consistently drinking water.  However, I am concerned about financial aspects of this proposal; will it be more difficult to implement in areas where there is lower income or less funding available to the public?

It is a great idea to have better drinking water standards and regulations base on facts. I do see the need for public education and information about what the people drink everyday. Though it is interesting that the United States is still at 10 ppb for lead and copper and allows higher containments for water sources. However, the notion of imputing standards is a step in the right direction and hopefully the United States can be in compliance with most of the developed country's 5 ppb rule, even with money being an issue and an understandable one.

I believe the proposed rule will increase public safety. No one wants lead or copper in their drinking water. The first step should definitely be more research into what is safe and acceptable levels. The proposal to include new requirements for drinking water testing is a necessity. We must protect the children in schools and child care facilities to make sure we are not exposing them to dangerous levels of toxins.
We concur generally with the comments on inadequate enforcement of the lead and copper rule by Cynthia Giles.

EPA's proposal to revise the Drinking Water State Revolving Fund (DWSRF) would require more federal funds to replace Lead Service Lines to protect public health. Many existing programs such as the Water Infrastructure Finance and Innovation Act (WIFIA) and more under the Water Infrastructure Improvements for the Nation Act all provide federal resources, and are the only sources of funding in replacing lead service lines. However, these programs are far from adequate in meeting EPA's need to replace the lines and thus could potentially cause local communities to raise water rates for more funding. While I support EPA's proposal to improve public health, this proposal has unrealistic goals in terms of funding the replacement of Lead Service Lines. The EPA should request for additional funding from Congress in order to move ahead.

The proposed changes in rules regarding lead and copper levels in drinking water is a welcome change to see. The tightening of restrictions on actionable levels and trigger levels will hopefully encourage municipalities and water service providers to update their service lines, and install new filtration services to keep our water clean. Between the crisis in Flint, and the more recent headlines regarding tainted water in Philadelphia, Miami, and New Orleans, seeing the EPA taking an interest in updating drinking water safety protocol is very reassuring. While these proposed rules regarding mostly the filtration of lead and copper, and subsequently the replacement of deteriorating lines contributing to those metal levels in the water, I personally would hope that the required actions these rules proscribe would give suppliers and municipalities a chance to implement even further filtration and cleaning services, while they are updating and replacing anyway.
