There is no safe level of lead exposure, yet outdated and faulty water delivery systems continue to expose millions of U.S. residents to lead in their drinking water. EPA has stated the need to modernize and improve the existing Lead and Copper Rule to increase public protections against the dangers posed by lead in drinking water in communities throughout the U.S. (1,2). The proposed revisions focus on a variety of factors including identification of lead in drinking water, methods of implementation of protective or corrective actions, replacement of lead service lines (LSLs) bringing water into homes, schools and day care centers, and notification and education of the public on appropriate actions to take.

As stated by the EPA and other organizations, the primary problem with lead contamination in drinking water lies with the estimated 6 million LSLs bringing contaminated water to residents of the U.S. (2,3). The proposed rule, however, would actually slow the replacement of lead-contaminated LSLs and thus increase the time frame over which residents could, and likely would, be exposed to lead (4). The existing rule calls for 7% of LSLs to be replaced annually in community water systems if lead measurements reach EPA's Action Level of >15 ppb. In contrast, the new rule calls for only 3% of LSLs to be replaced when >15 ppb lead is found. This change would effectively more than double the time for replacing offending LSLs for a given water system. A separate assessment by the Natural Resources Defense Council estimated that replacement of all LSLs could take 13 years under the existing rule, but up to 33 years under the revision (5). This assessment called for setting a maximum of 10 years to replace all LSLs nationwide. The American water industry believes that locating and removing all LSLs is urgently necessary (2,5). We agree that achieving this within 10 years is a worthy goal.

It is disconcerting that EPA has chosen not to decrease its Action Level of 15 ppb lead in drinking water. Although EPA has proposed decreasing the Trigger Level to 10 ppb (the level at which water systems must develop plans for the possible reaching of the 15 ppb Action Level in the future), the World Health Organization has stated that 10 ppb is not safe for the most vulnerable, notably children and pregnant mothers and their fetuses (2,5). Moreover, Canada has recently reduced its allowable lead level at the tap, its maximum contaminant level (MCL), to 5 ppb, and Europe has recommended reducing its MCL from 10 to 5 ppb. The NRDC has urged the EPA to lower its lead Action Level to 5 ppm (5). We strongly support this change.

The singular reason for reducing allowable lead concentrations in water now is to prevent exposure to lead, rather than wait for elevated lead to be detected and then for remedial actions to be instituted (5). Remedial actions take time. LSLs are removed only when such remedial actions are ineffective in reducing lead sufficiently. People are exposed in the meantime. The effects of these exposures can last a lifetime (6).

It is widely accepted that there is no safe level of exposure to lead. Yet, over the years 2015-2018, an estimated 5.5 million U.S. residents were served by water systems exceeding the 15 ppm Action Level, and an estimated 30 million residents received water from systems that were in violation of the LCR in a variety of ways. It is time to prevent another generation of children from growing up drinking lead contaminated water in their homes and schools (2,4).

(1)    www.epa.gov/newsreleases/epa-proposes-updates-lead-and-copper-rule-better-protect-children-and-risk-0

(2)  www.sciline.org/evidence-blog/lead-drinking-water

(3) www.washingtonpost.com/climate-environment/2019/10/10/first-time-decades-epa-is-overhauling-how-communities-must-test-lead-water/

(4)  www.nytimes.com/2019/10/10/climate/epa-lead-water-pipes.html

(5)   www.nrdc.org/media/2019/191010; www.nrdc.org/experts/erik-d-olson/heres-whats-needed-fix-outdated-epa-lead-tap-water-rule

(6)    CDC's Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report August 10, 2012, 61(04): 1-9.

(7)   Lead Poisoning, Annual Review of Medicine, 55: 209-222, 2004

National Primary Drinking Water Regulations: Proposed Lead and Copper Rule Revisions

City of Fort Wayne Utilities supports the comments posted by AWWA.  
In addition to the comments that AWWA presented, the City would also like to comment on and/or ask a few questions about the rule.  Please see below:

1.		On page 4 the rule states the following: "Prohibits sampling instructions that include recommendations for aerator cleaning/removal and pre-stagnation flushing prior to sample collection."
	Suggest clarifying that this language is only in relation to sample collecting.  We believe it is still a good idea for a utility to encourage cleaning aerators and flushing as good ways to mitigate lead during everyday use.  

2.		On page 16 and 17 the rule states the following:  "The water system would be in violation of the LCR treatment technique if it fails to meet the annual three percent full replacement rate unless the water system obtains documented refusals from all customers served by an LSL to participate in the replacement program. 	We believe that there are some portion of property owners who will not respond to the utility on this issue no matter how the utility tries to reach them.  We recommend that we treat unresponsive property owners that have documented outreach as refusals. 

3.	       Page 14 of the pdf states, states the following: "Water systems would be required to collect a follow up tap sample after three months but no later than six months after the gooseneck, pigtails, or connector is replaced."  
     Should an exemption be allowed for unresponsive customers in conformance with other areas of the rule?


City of Fort Wayne Utilities appreciates the general direction that the new lead and copper rule is trying to steer utilities towards.  Thank you for taking the time to read and address our comments.

Comments on Proposed Lead and Copper Rule Revisions

Lead Service Line Inventory - The task of creating a LSL inventory will require significant resources and staff to accomplish.  Additional guidance which clearly identifies and simplifies the means and methods to be used for the initial classification of lateral materials should be provided.  Also, appropriate guidance should be established regarding the "service lateral of unknown material" label, being aware that the "unknown material" label could result in unnecessary concern over water quality by consumers that conclude that a lead problem exists simply because the material of the lateral is unknown.  This is problematic particularly in regions where lead service lines are not as common.  The resulting inventory and subsequent mapping could be misconstrued if a majority of the laterals are labeled "unknown."  Guidance and materials to be provided to customers should be written accordingly.  Clarify what information is to be public on a water system's website and in what form (table, map, addresses).  

Water Sampling Requirements -  The rule revision requires schools and child care facilities to be sampled.  Those specific facilities (not the water system) should be required to perform and submit the sampling for compliance.  If they do not chose to collect their own samples, provide guidance for a private company to complete the testing for them.  Requiring water system personnel to enter private property is problematic.  Guidance and clarification is needed to specify the next steps ("find and fix") for a school or childcare facility that receives a sample result indicating lead.  Water systems should not incur costs to fix internal plumbing on private property.  Sampling additional taps in the facility, replacement of fixtures, and investigation of plumbing components inside the building would be reasonable steps based on findings from recent studies.  Additional guidance is also needed on a water system's responsibility for testing out in the system upon notification that a specific school or childcare facility has received a sample indicating lead.      

Economic Impact - This rule revision presents multiple requirements that will significantly increase costs to the water system.  Funding from the EPA must be provided to the water systems to offset the costs to hire additional staff and to assist with the increased sampling and testing that is anticipated in order to implement the lead service line inventory and follow up program.

3) The rule as proposed will create a tremendous amount of work and for public water systems and their employees (not to mention the expense for the water systems).  I believe this rule goes too far.  It doesn't seem to include much common sense.  We do not have control over the homeowners plumbing yet we are being held responsible for it.

I think that this is something really good and necessary for the safety of our people. No one should ever have to worry about the safety of their water especially not in a country as rich and developed as the US. The part that is especially appreciated is the part about education on the topic and why this might've been an issue in the first place. It is really important for people to understand the "why" of a situation especially when it could impact their lives directly.

I oppose this rule change, because it is inadequate to address the scope of the problem of lead-contaminated water.  These proposed rules allow lead-contaminated water systems to replace lead service lines more slowly than existing rules -- 3% replacement per year, as opposed to the current 7%.  This is unacceptable and poses a significant public health risk.  Moreover, the current rules do not set an aggressive enough standard for full replacement of lead service lines.  EPA should adopt Michigan's standard of replacing all lead service lines within 20 years. This goal is achievable, and the cost of replacement is lower than the costs of lead-contaminated water borne by the public.

US EPA Office of Water,
While an update to the LCR is overdue, what has been proposed still falls short of fixing the actual problems with this now almost 30 year-old rule. Copper corrosion will still not be accurately assessed by this rule proposal as the targeting approach has been updated to focus on some of the oldest residences in a water system. The science is clear that copper pipe ages and over time releases less copper to the water, therefore the highest risk for consumers and copper is in residences with fresh copper pipes (typically newer construction). These homes will not be targeted based on the current proposed rule language. Further EPA needs to define what "optimization" means, this is currently an un-measurable objective as there is no definition that states can universally use to deem a system optimized or determine that a system needs re-optimization. 

In regards to the first liter vs 5th liter samples, while the first liter is indeed generally not representative of water that has stagnated within the LSL, the 5th liter is not necessarily representative of the service line (this will vary from city to city and house to house). If EPA wants to consider the 5th liter approach they should consider a sampling approach similar to what the State of Michigan has employed for their lead and copper sampling. Where both a first and 5th liter sample are collected and the highest value is used to calculate the 90th percentile. EPA should require systems with LSLs to collect water that is representative of the LSL, this is the highest risk reservoir of lead to consumers when present. 

EPA should not require the testing of schools and daycares as part of the LCR. Many states have already implemented some school and daycare testing utilizing the 3Ts guidelines. This additional requirement in the LCR does not bolster those efforts and instead is a weaker version of the 3Ts guidance. In the 3Ts it is recommended that all outlets be tested, and in the LCR proposal it is mentioned that only 5 outlets in schools and 2 outlets in daycares be tested, this is an arbitrary number. This allows for gaming of the system and limits the ability to find-and-fix problems with faucets and outlets. Why require such a limited testing that really provides little to no information to the school/daycare and creates another burden on water systems?

EPA should provide justification for why the concentrations of 1 mg/L and 3 mg/L orthophosphate are to be evaluated in a corrosion control study. There are no citations associated with these requirements, why those two concentrations?

EPA should consider how various water quality zones in a water system affect corrosion. Large drinking water distribution systems with multiple consecutive systems and various interconnections often have areas of varying water quality that are known by the operators. Therefore LCR sampling should be conducted in a way to represent these various water quality zones in order to protect consumers and accurately assess corrosion within these complex systems.
There is no safe lead of level for drinking water!
Do better.
We need all lead service lines replaced
The best way to prevent lead is to get rid of it entirely. Stop partial replacements of lead lines and start being proactive lead is already harming thousands of people and more to come.

Please consider the following comments and questions regarding the proposed Lead and Copper Rule revisions.  These comments are being submitted on behalf of the Conference of Environmental Health Directors (CEHD) of New York State.  Our organization is comprised of county, city, and regional environmental health directors, sanitarians and engineers that are responsible for ensuring public water systems are compliant with drinking water rules and regulations throughout the state of New York.  I am an Executive Board member of the CEHD, Director of Environmental Health for the Rockland County Department of Health, and a Licensed Professional Engineer in New York and New Jersey.  I have a bachelors degree in Chemical Engineering from Stevens Institute of Technology and a certificate in Public Health Management from Washington University.   I have 18+ years of experience working with drinking water rules and regulations including the Lead and Copper Rule.  The comments provided below have been compiled and selected from CEHD members and are being submitted on behalf of the CEHD Executive Board. 

While we agree that phosphorous loading at wastewater treatment plants should not be used to eliminate consideration of orthophosphate for CCT, discharge limits for wastewater should nonetheless be included in overall assessment of an optimal CCT design. 

The proposed rule states that the PWS must replace the utility-owned portion of a lead service line within 45 days of being advised the homeowner replaced their portion. This time frame does not take into account potential difficulties in acquiring road-opening permits, which can be an issue in winter months, and when a road surface has recently been repaved. 

The requirement for the utility to conduct find-and-fix actions should await follow-up sampling to verify an elevated lead result. This portion of the rule comprises a modified application of the numerical action level, which was originally defined to be applied to the 90th percentile as a measure of the overall efficacy of the CCT strategy, not as a health-based standard. It is now proposed to be used as a sample-specific standard. As such, it should include a requirement for a verification sample, similar to what would be required for determining compliance with an MCL 

An argument could be made to avoid single lever faucets for sample collection of LCR monitoring samples due to the potential for blending with hot water. However, we may want to stop short of disallowing them since their popularity may result in an inadequate sample pool. We recommend that sampling instructions state that single-lever faucets be avoided if possible, but if used, the sampler should make every effort to ensure the lever is fully toward the cold position. 

The investigation required to develop an accurate LSL inventory would be resource-intensive, in many cases requiring an excavation for each service line. As a result, it is not unlikely that the actual result will be similar to the current status, with many service lines simply being designated as "unknown." However, due to the related requirement for public education for service lines of unknown material as well as LSLs, a positive outcome may nevertheless be that homeowners are encouraged to determine whether "unknown" service lines are comprised of lead. 

We would like to confirm that a blended polyorthophosphate, which many  systems use, will still be acceptable. The proposed regulation would require CCT with phosphate to be orthophosphate based. We agree that a polyphosphate should not be used alone for lead and copper CCT. The proposed regulation mentions targeting a 1 mg/l orthophosphate level at taps within the system. Will a system that has a history of complying with the action level, while optimized at a lower othrophosphate target, be  required to raise targets to 1 mg/l?

We agree that achieving this within 10 years is a worthy goal.

It is disconcerting that EPA has chosen not to decrease its Action Level of 15 ppb lead in drinking water. Although EPA has proposed decreasing the Trigger Level to 10 ppb (the level at which water systems must develop plans for the possible reaching of the 15 ppb Action Level in the future), the World Health Organization has stated that 10 ppb is not safe for the most vulnerable, notably children and pregnant mothers and their fetuses (2,5). Moreover, Canada has recently reduced its allowable lead level at the tap, its maximum contaminant level (MCL), to 5 ppb, and Europe has recommended reducing its MCL from 10 to 5 ppb. The NRDC has urged the EPA to lower its lead Action Level to 5 ppm (5). We strongly support this change.

The singular reason for reducing allowable lead concentrations in water now is to prevent exposure to lead, rather than wait for elevated lead to be detected and then for remedial actions to be instituted (5). Remedial actions take time. LSLs are removed only when such remedial actions are ineffective in reducing lead sufficiently. People are exposed in the meantime. The effects of these exposures can last a lifetime (6).

It is widely accepted that there is no safe level of exposure to lead. Yet, over the years 2015-2018, an estimated 5.5 million U.S. residents were served by water systems exceeding the 15 ppm Action Level, and an estimated 30 million residents received water from systems that were in violation of the LCR in a variety of ways. It is time to prevent another generation of children from growing up drinking lead contaminated water in their homes and schools (2,4).
